title: Epic — Foundation: workspace, CI, release skeleton, fitness harness
description:
Cross-cutting work that must exist before Phase 1 can start: Cargo workspace,
crate boundaries, CI skeleton, release binary skeleton, architecture fitness
test harness, web/ pnpm scaffold, docs/adr scaffold, xtask runner, and the
cross-platform CI matrix.

PRD refs: §8.2 (workspace layout), §17.6 (architecture fitness tests),
§18 (distribution), §20.1 (CI checks), §22 (build sequence #1), §19.4
(repo/contributor docs), AGENTS.md §4 (invariants), §5 (toolchains).

Definition of done for this epic:
- All crates listed in PRD §8.2 exist as empty/stub library crates compiling
  on Linux, macOS, and Windows.
- `cargo xtask check` runs fmt + clippy + tests + fitness + fixtures and is
  green on a fresh checkout.
- `web/` builds with pnpm, frozen lockfile, pinned packageManager.
- Architecture fitness tests pass for the empty workspace and fail loudly if
  a banned dependency is added (see AGENTS.md §4.5).
- Release binary skeleton produces signed-or-checksummed artifacts in CI for
  Linux/macOS/Windows with `know-now version` smoke-test.
- `docs/adr/0000-template.md` and `docs/adr/README.md` exist; the ADR process
  is documented (PRD §19.4, AGENTS.md §7.1).

Children depend on this epic; Phase 1 cannot begin until the workspace and
fitness harness exist.

## Success Criteria

Close this epic only when its child beads prove the described scope, every phase or NFR gate is linked to evidence, and remaining follow-up work is explicitly deferred into separate beads.

title: Cargo workspace skeleton with all crates from PRD §8.2
description:
Create the Rust workspace and stub library crates listed in PRD §8.2 so that
later beads can land in the right place without churn.

PRD refs: §8.2 (workspace layout), §22 build sequence #1, AGENTS.md §5.

Crates to create as empty `[lib]` stubs (one Cargo.toml + lib.rs each, with
crate-level rustdoc that names the responsibility):

  crates/know_now_cli/           CLI entrypoint (also a [bin])
  crates/know_now_core/          orchestration, project loading, plans
  crates/know_now_diagnostics/   diagnostics, source spans, renderers
  crates/know_now_metadata/      metadata types, parsing, source spans
  crates/know_now_contract/      stable generator/API contract schemas
  crates/know_now_identity/      stable object IDs, suggestions, matching
  crates/know_now_validate/      semantic + policy validation
  crates/know_now_codegen/       generator traits + artifact model
  crates/know_now_ir/            typed SQL/DDL/docs IR
  crates/know_now_writer/        staging, path safety, atomic promote
  crates/know_now_lock/          lockfile schema + checks
  crates/know_now_gen_postgres/  PostgreSQL DDL generator
  crates/know_now_gen_dbt/       dbt generator
  crates/know_now_gen_quality/   quality contracts + dbt tests
  crates/know_now_gen_docs/      Markdown + Mermaid generation
  crates/know_now_diff/          graph diffing, ID matching, classification
  crates/know_now_server/        local axum API server
  crates/know_now_policy/        policy pack loading + evaluation
  crates/know_now_templates/     restricted MiniJinja-based template packs
  crates/know_now_cache/         content-addressed cache
  crates/know_now_toolchain/     external toolchain adapters (e.g., dbt)
  crates/know_now_audit/         audit events, redaction, support bundles
  xtask/                         release/fixture/benchmark/maintenance

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `cargo build --workspace` succeeds on a fresh checkout.
2. `cargo test --workspace` succeeds (empty tests are fine).
3. Each crate has a top-of-file rustdoc comment naming its responsibility
   and citing the PRD section that defines it.
4. workspace Cargo.toml uses `resolver = "2"`.
5. Crate names use snake_case to match PRD §8.2 exactly. Do not invent
   shorter aliases.
6. No crate yet pulls in serde_yaml/serde_yml/marked-yaml/serde-saphyr
   (parser choice happens in Phase 1).

Do NOT in this bead:
- Add real implementation. This is a scaffolding bead only.
- Add cross-crate dependencies that are not justified (NFR-M1: generator
  crates have no direct cross-deps).
- Add a workspace-wide dependency that breaks the parser-isolation rule
  (AGENTS.md §4.5 banned deps).

title: Structured logging + tracing infrastructure (tracing + JSON spans + --verbose/--debug)
description:
Cross-cutting structured logging + tracing infrastructure for the engine,
CLI, and local server. Without this, NFR-P12 (--verbose + JSON include
stage timings) and the per-phase E2E test scripts cannot assert on what
actually happened during a run.

PRD refs: NFR-P12 (pipeline timing breakdown — --verbose + JSON include
stage timings), §12.2 (standard flags --verbose / --debug), §12.4 (error
diagnostics — text/JSON renderers), §17.1 (stage budgets), §17.6
(architecture fitness), §8.2 (know_now_audit owns redaction +
support-bundle summaries), AGENTS.md §4.2 (volatile vs deterministic).

What this bead establishes (load-bearing for every later phase):
- Logging crate choice: tracing + tracing-subscriber (verify under
  cargo deny; isolate any C/FFI deps). Document the choice in an ADR
  (AGENTS.md §7.1). Banned alternatives: log/env_logger as the primary
  facade (env_logger is fine as a dev-only helper, never the product).
- Span/event taxonomy: one named span per pipeline stage from PRD §17.1
  (discovery, parsing, yaml-subset, deserialize, semantic, policy,
  default-resolution, contract, capabilities, planning, generation,
  validation, manual-edit-detection, path-safety, stale-plan, atomic-
  write, manifesting, run-log).
- Stage timing instrumentation: every span records start, end, and
  duration_ms; structured fields for stage name, project root hash
  (NOT path), generator name, artifact count.
- --verbose / --debug knobs (PRD §12.2): --verbose lifts logging to
  INFO; --debug to DEBUG; --no-color disables ANSI; --format json|sarif
  emits structured logs alongside diagnostics.
- JSON event format is schema-pinned under
  know_now_diagnostics::LOG_SCHEMA_VERSION so downstream tools (CI,
  dashboard, support bundle) can rely on it.
- Determinism boundary: log events MAY contain timestamps + run_id
  because they are volatile run state (NFR-R13, AGENTS.md §4.2);
  generated/ MUST NEVER include log content. Fitness-test enforces.
- Server (Phase 3) integration contract only: define request-log field names, redaction expectations, and test helpers here. Axum middleware, launch-token/security events, CORS/CSP reject logging, and X-Request-Id behavior are implemented by know-now-hrw.13/27 and consumed by the dashboard beads.
- Support bundle integration: .knownow/runs/<run_id>.json includes the
  structured event stream (after redaction) so support bundles can
  carry "what happened" without secrets (S_P3_SUPPORT, NFR-S18).
- Test affordance: every later test bead can capture the JSON event
  stream and assert on stage names, durations within budget, expected
  field values, and absence of secrets / timestamps in deterministic
  artifacts.

Crate placement (PRD §8.2):
- Logging facade lives in know_now_diagnostics (consumed by all crates).
- Redaction primitives live in know_now_audit::redaction
  (PRD §8.2: "audit events, redaction, support-bundle summaries"); the
  diagnostics layer's tracing subscriber registers a know_now_audit
  redaction layer in its pipeline so log events flow through redaction
  before serialization. This keeps redaction logic and patterns in
  one place and lets hrw.8 (support bundle) and hrw.24 (audit log)
  reuse the same primitives.
- Architecture fitness test: know_now_audit must NOT depend on
  know_now_diagnostics (avoid the cycle); know_now_diagnostics may
  depend on know_now_audit.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. tracing + tracing-subscriber added to know_now_diagnostics; cargo
   deny green; ADR recorded under docs/adr/.
2. know_now_audit::redaction primitives implemented with patterns for
   high-entropy / AWS-style / PEM / $HOME / hostname / username
   leakage; documented in docs/dev/security.md.
3. Stage spans listed above are emitted in INFO at minimum. Each stage
   span is unit-tested for presence on a representative pipeline run.
4. --verbose, --debug, --format json|sarif, --no-color flags are wired
   on the global CLI argument layer (S_P2A_CLI) and propagate to library
   crates via a single configured subscriber (no global mutable state
   inside library crates; libraries take a Subscriber handle).
5. NFR-P12 verified: a unit test asserts that --verbose --format json
   on a small fixture emits one JSON event per stage from the §17.1 list,
   each carrying duration_ms and stage name, sorted by start time but
   stable per identical input (deterministic ordering after duration
   redaction in tests).
6. NFR-R13 verified by fitness test: no path from generated/ writers can
   leak log content into generated/manifest.json or any deterministic
   artifact (architecture fitness #4 + a content-scan integration test).
7. Secret-redaction unit tests: log events containing values matching
   high-entropy / AWS-style / PEM patterns are redacted before emission
   (NFR-S18 / NFR-S12). Patterns documented in docs/dev/security.md.
8. Server integration boundary documented: this bead defines request-id span field names, validation rules, redaction expectations, and TestSubscriber helpers only; axum middleware and X-Request-Id behavior are implemented by know-now-hrw.13 and verified by know-now-hrw.27.
9. Build-time logging regression test: a banned-pattern fitness check
   refuses println! / eprintln! in any production crate
   (test crates and xtask exempted) so log calls go through the
   tracing facade.
10. Documentation: docs/dev/logging.md describes the span taxonomy,
    field names, JSON schema version, redaction patterns, and how to
    add a new span without breaking the schema.

Do NOT:
- Use println!/eprintln! in production code paths (fitness test
  enforces; tests + xtask exempted).
- Mutate global subscriber state from library crates — accept a handle.
- Log secrets, env vars, or absolute home paths (NFR-S9, NFR-S12,
  NFR-S18).
- Embed log timestamps inside generated/ or generated/manifest.json
  (AGENTS.md §4.2, NFR-R13).
- Couple the log JSON schema to any specific phase. The schema is
  versioned across phases; downstream tools pin to LOG_SCHEMA_VERSION.
- Place redaction patterns in know_now_diagnostics (they belong in
  know_now_audit per PRD §8.2). Diagnostics consumes redaction; it
  does not own it.

Cross-references:
- NFR-P12 stage timings consumed by S_BENCHMARKS (know-now-bsf.1).
- NFR-S18 secret redaction consumed by support bundle (know-now-hrw.8)
  and audit log (know-now-hrw.24).
- E2E test beads (Phase 1: upe.14, Phase 2A: 63g.18, Phase 2B: 8vf.9,
  Phase 3: hrw.27/.28/.29) ALL depend on this bead so their assertions
  can read structured events.
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; testing + log-shape rigor):

Output stream conventions:
- All structured events go to stderr (NEVER stdout). stdout is reserved
  for command results so that `--format json | jq …` pipelines remain
  clean. Verified by an integration test: every command's stdout, when
  --format text, contains zero JSON-event lines; --format json on
  stdout contains the result envelope only; events always on stderr.
- Pretty-printed text is the default on a TTY. JSON-Lines on a non-TTY
  unless --format text is explicit. --no-color disables ANSI even on a
  TTY.

Cross-OS log-shape stability:
- events.jsonl shape (stage names + field presence — NEVER timestamp
  or duration values) committed under
  tests/logging/expected_event_shape.schema.json (JSON Schema). CI
  validates a captured stream from a deterministic fixture against the
  schema on Linux + macOS + Windows.
- Stage-name constants live in know_now_diagnostics::stage and are
  exported as a public enum so test fixtures cannot drift from
  production names without a compile error.

Redaction fuzz (mirrored under bsf.2):
- 10000+ randomly generated secret-shaped strings (deterministic seed
  under tests/security/secret_patterns/) injected through every
  CLI command + server endpoint + audit path. Assert zero leak in
  events.jsonl, audit log, support bundle, generated/, manifest.json,
  stderr.
- Pattern catalog covers: high-entropy >= 32 chars, AWS-style
  AKIA[A-Z0-9]+ + 40-char secret, JWT-shape three-segment dot, PEM
  -----BEGIN…-----, env-var KEY=VALUE shapes, $HOME / username /
  hostname leakage.

Banned production macros (fitness test):
- println!, eprintln!, dbg!, print!, eprint! are forbidden in every
  production crate (allowed only under tests/ + xtask/). The clippy
  lint table in workspace Cargo.toml denies these; the fitness test
  greps for compiled-in usage as a belt-and-braces gate.

Server correlation contract (implemented by hrw.13, verified by hrw.27):
- Define request_id field names, validation rules (ASCII, max 64 chars), redaction expectations, and helper assertions. Do not implement axum middleware in this foundation bead; the server beads own runtime HTTP behavior.

Test affordances exposed to later beads:
- TestSubscriber: a tracing-subscriber capture layer that returns a
  Vec<JsonEvent> for in-process assertions (used by upe.14, 63g.18,
  8vf.9, hrw.27/28/29).
- assert_stage_sequence!() macro that takes the captured events and
  asserts the §17.1 stage span sequence appeared in deterministic
  order with required fields.
- assert_no_secrets!() macro that runs the redaction-pattern catalog
  over a captured event stream.
close_reason: Completed — commit bcf98a5 pushed to main

title: Foundation E2E + xtask check smoke on fresh checkout
description:
The canonical \"is the foundation healthy?\" E2E driver. Without this,
every Phase 1 / 2A / 2B / 3 driver assumes the foundation works, but
nothing actively verifies a fresh contributor checkout boots end-to-end.

PRD refs: §17.6 (architecture fitness tests), §18.2 (contributor setup),
§20.1 (CI checks), §22 build sequence #1, AGENTS.md §4 (invariants),
§5.1 (common commands).

Why this bead exists:
- 42e.1..42e.9 build the foundation surfaces (workspace, toolchain,
  CI, fitness, release skeleton, web scaffold, xtask, ADR, cross-OS
  matrix).
- 42e.10 establishes the structured-logging facade.
- This bead is the integration test that ALL of those work together
  on a clean clone, before the Phase 1 spike (upe.*) is allowed to
  start.

Driver entry point:
  cargo xtask e2e foundation

What the driver does (each step emits a tracing span via 42e.10):

1. Fresh-checkout simulation: snapshot current git tree + run from a
   throw-away directory under .knownow/staging/foundation_e2e/ so
   side effects are contained.
2. cargo build --workspace                 — every stub crate compiles.
3. cargo test --workspace                  — empty tests succeed.
4. cargo fmt --all -- --check              — formatter clean.
5. cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings.
6. cargo deny check                        — license + advisory +
                                              banned-deps.
7. Architecture fitness tests run against the empty workspace and
   surface every rule from PRD §17.6 (parser-isolation, generator
   independence, writer single-write-path, banned macros, etc.).
   Adding a banned dep (test fixture flips a feature flag) MUST cause
   the fitness suite to fail loudly.
8. cd web && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm typecheck &&
   pnpm build                              — frontend boot.
9. Beads graph hygiene:
   - br dep cycles (must report 0 cycles).
   - br lint (template completeness).
   - br --no-db sync --status (no DB-vs-JSONL drift).
10. Logging assertions on the captured events.jsonl from steps 1-9:
    - Every step emits a stage span with stable name + duration_ms
      (NFR-P12).
    - No secret patterns leak (calls into bsf.2's pattern catalog).
    - No log content leaks into generated/ or generated/manifest.json
      (NFR-R13).
11. Cross-OS smoke: same xtask runs on Linux + macOS + Windows
    (NFR-PO1, NFR-R1). Path normalization + line endings asserted.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. cargo xtask e2e foundation runs in <8 minutes on Linux from a
   clean checkout (budget; promote to hard SLA when baseline
   stabilizes).
2. Cross-OS green on Linux + macOS + Windows (and WSL paths from
   42e.9).
3. events.jsonl shape committed under
   tests/e2e/foundation/expected_log_shape.schema.json.
4. Fixture project for the banned-dep regression test lives under
   tests/fitness/banned_dep_regression/ and is exercised by step 7.
5. Failure output dumps: events.jsonl, stdout/stderr per step,
   .knownow/staging/foundation_e2e/ contents — uploaded as CI
   artifacts on failure.
6. Documented in docs/dev/e2e/foundation.md: how to run locally,
   how to interpret a failure, how to add a new foundation gate.

Do NOT:
- Make this bead a duplicate of cargo xtask check. xtask check is
  the developer ergonomics command; this is the IS-IT-WORKING
  integration driver that adds events.jsonl assertions, cross-OS
  matrix, and the fresh-checkout simulation.
- Skip any of steps 1-11 because \"that's covered elsewhere\". The
  whole point is one place that asserts the entire foundation works.
- Use println!/eprintln! anywhere in this driver — go through
  42e.10's tracing facade, same as production code.

Dependencies (wired below):
- Parent: 42e (Foundation epic).
- Blocked by: 42e.1, 42e.2, 42e.3, 42e.4, 42e.6, 42e.7, 42e.10.
  (42e.5 release skeleton + 42e.8 ADR + 42e.9 cross-OS CI are
  parallel; they don't block this driver.)
- Blocks: upe.13 (Phase 1 exit-criteria audit). Phase 1 audit cannot
  ship without the foundation E2E being green.
close_reason: Completed — 9-step foundation E2E driver + schema + fixture + docs

title: rust-toolchain.toml + clippy + cargo deny + fmt config pinned
description:
Pin the Rust toolchain and standard lint/audit tooling so that every
contributor and CI agent runs the exact same checks.

PRD refs: AGENTS.md §5, §6.1, NFR-M4 (CI runs formatting/linting/type
checking/tests/dependency policy checks), NFR-S5 (Rust deps audited for
licenses/advisories/duplicates/banned packages).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `rust-toolchain.toml` pins a stable channel + components: rustfmt,
   clippy. Document in AGENTS.md §5 once the version is chosen.
2. `rustfmt.toml` defines house style (edition = "2021" or current);
   `cargo fmt --all -- --check` is enforced by CI.
3. Clippy config: `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   passes; lint level overrides documented in workspace Cargo.toml `lints`
   table.
4. `deny.toml` configures cargo-deny: license allowlist, advisory database
   gate, duplicate dep detection, banned-deps list (serde_yaml, serde_yml,
   any C/FFI YAML parser, any unrestricted minijinja for *custom* template
   packs — AGENTS.md §4.5).
5. Banned-dep list is enforced (CI fails if a banned crate appears in the
   workspace).
6. Verify with: `cargo deny check`.

Do NOT:
- Skip clippy or cargo-deny in CI for any reason (AGENTS.md §8 hard don't).
- Add `--no-verify` workarounds.

title: CI skeleton — fmt/clippy/test/coverage matrix
description:
Stand up the GitHub-Actions-or-equivalent CI pipeline that every later bead
depends on. The pipeline must encode the PRD's required checks so that an
implementer cannot land a bead that violates an architecture invariant.

PRD refs: §20.1 (CI checks), §20.4 (compatibility matrix), NFR-M4, NFR-PO1.

Required CI checks (PRD §20.1):
- Rust formatting (cargo fmt --check).
- Rust linting (cargo clippy -D warnings).
- Rust tests (cargo test --workspace).
- Rust coverage (>=90% overall, >=95% for know_now_metadata + codegen +
  writer — NFR-R2). May start as a soft gate, then promote to hard once
  baseline is established.
- TypeScript typecheck (`pnpm typecheck` under web/ — wait for web stub).
- TypeScript lint/test/build.
- Dependency policy checks (cargo deny check).
- License checks (folded into cargo-deny).
- Snapshot tests.
- Property-based tests for metadata validation and diff classification.
- Mutation-style tests for policy pack rules.
- Generated DDL execution against PostgreSQL (matrix: PG 16/17/18 — see
  PRD §11.2 compatibility floor).
- dbt compile for generated demo project where dbt validation is configured
  (Phase 2B).
- Mermaid syntax validation (Phase 2B).
- JSON Schema validation.
- Benchmark suite (NFR-P10: >20% regression fails CI unless approved).
- Architecture fitness tests (NFR-M5, see separate bead).
- Cross-platform test matrix (Linux/macOS/Windows — NFR-PO1).
- Compatibility fixture diff suite (Phase 2B+).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. CI runs on every push to main and on every commit-to-main staging
   request (per AGENTS.md §7.6 trunk-based workflow).
2. Required checks are listed in the workflow file and enforced as
   branch protection (where the platform allows).
3. CI failure produces a single, clear summary linking to the failing
   step.
4. Job runtime budget: keep the matrix tight — Linux + macOS + Windows
   for Rust tests, Linux only for benchmark + DDL execution where
   external services are required.

Do NOT:
- Skip git hooks or signing on CI commits (AGENTS.md §8).
- Add long-running optional jobs that gate every PR; put expensive
  validations behind a labeled trigger or scheduled run.
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; planning-graph hygiene + workflow gates):

Beads graph hygiene checks (run on every push that touches .beads/):
- `br dep cycles` — must report zero cycles. Fails CI if any cycle is
  detected.
- `br lint` — template completeness check on issues. Surfaces missing
  acceptance criteria, stale templates, etc.
- `br --no-db sync --status` — must report no drift between the
  SQLite DB and the committed JSONL. Prevents accidental DB-only
  changes from sneaking past review.
- `br stats` JSON sanity: a CI step parses `br stats --json` and asserts
  invariants such as \"Total Issues - Tombstones >= 0\", \"Ready > 0
  unless every epic is closed\".

Workflow / docs gates:
- AGENTS.md link validation: a markdown-link-check step verifies every
  internal link in AGENTS.md, README.md, and docs/PRD.md resolves.
- ADR index sync: docs/adr/README.md must reference every file under
  docs/adr/ that is not the template; fails if drift detected.

Cross-cutting test suite gates (consolidated from per-phase beads):
- Compatibility-fixture diff suite produces a JSON summary used by
  release-note classification (PRD §20.2). CI artifact uploaded on
  every run; classification enforced on tagged releases.
- E2E aggregator: CI calls `cargo xtask e2e all` (Linux only) and
  `cargo xtask e2e <phase>` for each active phase on macOS + Windows
  to keep matrix runtime in budget while preserving cross-OS coverage
  (NFR-PO1, NFR-R1).
- Logging schema validator: `cargo xtask logs validate` runs in CI
  against captured fixtures from each phase E2E.
- Redaction fuzz: `cargo xtask redaction fuzz` runs nightly (avoid
  blocking PRs); deterministic seed snapshotted so failures reproduce
  locally.

Acceptance additions:
5. CI workflow file emits a single-page summary linking to: Rust
   coverage report, fixture diff summary, benchmark trend, fitness
   test report, beads graph hygiene report. Maintainer sees one place
   for \"is the project healthy?\".
6. CI fails fast on any invariant in `br stats` (Open == 0 with no
   closed beads is suspicious; signal as warning rather than fail).
7. CI integration with mcp-agent-mail pre-commit guard (AGENTS.md
   §7.4 + §8): pre-commit hook checks for unreleased reservations
   and stale thread state. Repository-level CI verifies the guard
   wiring is present in every contributor checkout.

Beads viewer routing smoke (plan-space guard):
- CI records `br ready --json`, `bv --robot-triage`, and `bv --robot-plan` output when .beads/ changes. It may also record `bv --robot-next` as a known-regression signal, but `br ready --json` is the authoritative ready set.
- Parent-child links are rollups, not implementation blockers. If `br ready --json` reports ready implementation beads but `bv --robot-next` reports none, the CI summary must flag the mismatch and point maintainers at the graph/tooling issue before implementation starts.
- Until the mismatch is fixed in the pinned viewer/tooling, agents must route with `br ready --json` and use `bv --robot-plan` only for graph context. AGENTS.md and CONTRIBUTING.md must say this before closing this bead.

Acceptance additions:
8. The beads hygiene report makes routing failures visible enough that an implementer never has to guess the next bead from chat.
9. The CI/docs gate explicitly proves the active trunk branch name used by workflow files matches AGENTS.md/README.md, so `main` vs temporary branch-name drift cannot silently confuse contributors.

title: Architecture fitness test harness
description:
Build the fitness-test crate (or xtask subcommand) that enforces the
architecture invariants in AGENTS.md §4 and PRD §17.6 mechanically. These
tests run in CI and block any change that breaks an invariant.

PRD refs: §17.6 (architecture fitness tests), NFR-M5, AGENTS.md §4 + §8.

Mechanism: walk the workspace `Cargo.toml` files (and the generated dep
graph from `cargo metadata`) and assert facts. Use `cargo_metadata` crate
or equivalent. Test failures should print a clear human-readable message
naming the violated invariant and the offending crate.

Invariants the harness must enforce (PRD §17.6):
1. Generator crates (know_now_gen_*) do not depend on YAML parser crates
   (serde-saphyr, marked-yaml, saphyr-parser, serde_yaml, serde_yml).
2. YAML parser dependencies are isolated to know_now_metadata.
3. Generator crates only consume validated graph/generator-contract inputs
   (i.e., depend on know_now_contract + know_now_codegen + know_now_ir,
   never on know_now_metadata directly).
4. Built-in generators do not write files directly — they depend on
   know_now_codegen (artifact-descriptor traits), not on know_now_writer
   internals; only know_now_writer (and through it know_now_core) writes.
5. All writes go through the artifact writer.
6. Artifact writer does not depend on individual generator crates
   (know_now_writer must NOT have know_now_gen_* in its dep tree).
7. Diagnostics can be emitted in text/JSON/SARIF without
   generator-specific code (know_now_diagnostics has no generator deps).
8. Lockfile resolution is isolated from artifact generation
   (know_now_lock independent of know_now_gen_* and know_now_writer).
9. Policy validation cannot mutate metadata (know_now_policy must not
   expose &mut access to ProjectGraph or Authoring* types).
10. Local server write endpoints are disabled unless explicitly enabled
    (know_now_server: feature flag or runtime guard verified via test).
11. Dashboard/server API contract compatibility is tested in CI.
12. Template packs cannot write files directly.
13. know_now_templates produces artifact descriptors and does not bypass
    writer path-safety rules.
14. Template rendering cannot access raw YAML parser types.
15. Custom template packs cannot register native MiniJinja functions,
    filters, tests, or loaders (compile-time API gate, not just docs).
16. Unsupported renderer profile changes fail compatibility tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Each invariant has a named test that fails with a human message
   identifying the violated rule and the crate at fault.
2. The harness runs as part of `cargo xtask check` and as a discrete CI
   step.
3. Negative-control test: deliberately add a banned dep in a separate
   branch (or simulate via a fixture) to confirm the harness fails loudly.
4. A README in the harness crate explains how to add a new invariant.

Do NOT:
- Encode invariants only in human docs. The fitness harness IS the
  enforcement layer.
- Use `#[ignore]` to skip a fitness test under any circumstances.

title: Release binary skeleton (Linux/macOS/Windows) + cargo install fallback
description:
Stand up the release pipeline that produces signed binaries, checksums, and
package metadata for cargo-binstall. Even before know-now does anything
useful, this skeleton must work so that the supply-chain story is built
into the project from day one.

PRD refs: §18 (distribution), §20.3 (release artifacts), NFR-SS1..SS7,
§22 build sequence #37.

Release outputs (PRD §20.3):
- Linux binary (gnu + musl).
- macOS binary (x86_64 + aarch64).
- Windows binary (x86_64 MSVC).
- Checksums (SHA-256) for every binary/archive.
- Artifact attestations where practical (SLSA / sigstore).
- SBOMs where practical (cyclonedx / spdx).
- Crate release.
- Docker image where appropriate.
- Changelog.
- Migration notes for metadata/generator contract changes.
- Generated fixture diff summary if outputs changed.
- cargo-binstall-compatible package metadata where practical
  (`[package.metadata.binstall]`).
- Installation verification snippets per platform.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Release CI is triggered by tag push from main only (no draft tags from
   forks).
2. Binaries are built in CI from tagged commits (not locally uploaded).
3. SHA-256 checksums published for every binary/archive.
4. cargo-binstall metadata in workspace Cargo.toml validated by a release
   smoke test.
5. Source-build fallback documented as
   `cargo install --locked know-now --version <version>` and verified by
   release CI on each supported OS.
6. Release CI runs `know-now version` against each install path
   (direct binary, cargo binstall, cargo install) and fails the release
   if any is broken.
7. Lockfiles committed and verified for release builds (NFR-S10).
8. Attestations + SBOMs published where practical (capture the "where
   practical" call-out as a documented decision in docs/dev or ADR).

Do NOT:
- Bundle dashboard build artifacts under web/dist/ into the repo
  (AGENTS.md §8 — they are release assets, not source).
- Skip checksum verification in any install path.
- Use `--no-verify` or `--no-gpg-sign` in release commits without an
  explicit, recorded reason.
close_reason: Completed — release workflow + binstall metadata + docs

title: web/ pnpm scaffold — pinned packageManager + frozen-lockfile CI
description:
Bootstrap the TypeScript/React/Vite dashboard package layout under web/.
Even though the dashboard itself is Phase 3, the scaffold + lockfile policy
must be in place from day one so that frontend additions follow a single
discipline.

PRD refs: §8.2 (web/ subtree), §18.2 (contributor setup), §19.4 (repo
docs), NFR-I9, NFR-M3, NFR-M7, AGENTS.md §5, §8.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `web/package.json` exists with:
   - `"name": "@know-now/dashboard"`,
   - `"private": true`,
   - `"type": "module"`,
   - `"packageManager": "pnpm@<exact-pinned-version>"` (NFR-I9),
   - `"engines": {"node": ">=<version-required-by-current-vite>"}`,
   - scripts: `dev` (vite), `typecheck` (tsc --noEmit), `test` (vitest run),
     `build` (vite build), `lint`.
2. `web/pnpm-lock.yaml` exists, committed, and used by CI with
   `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (NFR-M7).
3. CI installs/activates the pinned pnpm version explicitly (do NOT rely on
   Corepack being bundled — see PRD §18.2 frontend rules).
4. `web/tsconfig.json` runs in strict mode (NFR-M3); recommended additional
   flags: `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`,
   `noImplicitOverride` (AGENTS.md §6.2).
5. `web/.gitignore` excludes `node_modules/`, `dist/` (AGENTS.md §8 — never
   commit web/dist/).
6. No `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `bun.lock`, or `bun.lockb` under
   `web/`.
7. CI fails if any non-pnpm lockfile appears in `web/`.

Do NOT:
- Use `npm` or `yarn` commands in CI.
- Add a TypeScript dependency that can't be resolved frozen-lockfile.
- Add `"any"` types without an explanation comment (AGENTS.md §6.2).

title: xtask runner — check/fixture/benchmark/release subcommands
description:
The `xtask` crate is the maintainer-facing task runner. Defines a single
entry point (`cargo xtask check`) that runs the same checks CI runs, plus
maintenance tasks (fixture regen, benchmark, release).

PRD refs: §8.2 (xtask listed in workspace), §18.2 (contributor setup),
AGENTS.md §5.1.

Subcommands (initial set):
- `cargo xtask check` — fmt + clippy + cargo-deny + tests + fitness +
  fixture diff. This is the canonical "did I break anything" command for
  contributors.
- `cargo xtask fixtures regen` — regenerate all snapshot/fixture outputs.
  Must require a flag like --confirm to avoid accidental noise.
- `cargo xtask fixtures diff` — produce a fixture diff summary suitable
  for the release notes (PRD §20.2).
- `cargo xtask bench` — run the benchmark suite (NFR-P10 budget gate).
- `cargo xtask release prepare <version>` — version bumps + changelog
  prep + cargo-binstall metadata sync.
- `cargo xtask docs build` — build user docs site (Phase 3+).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. xtask is a `[bin]` in the workspace; not pulled into the released
   crate's dep tree.
2. `cargo xtask check` runs in <5 min on the empty workspace and prints a
   single PASS/FAIL summary at the end.
3. `cargo xtask fixtures diff` exits non-zero when fixtures differ;
   the diff is human-readable and JSON-serializable.
4. xtask uses no GitHub-only paths — must work locally and on any CI.

Do NOT:
- Encode CI logic in shell scripts that diverge from xtask. xtask is the
  source of truth; CI calls xtask, not the other way around.
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; aggregate test/log/security task runners):

Additional subcommands:

- `cargo xtask e2e <phase>` — run the canonical phase E2E driver.
  Phases: foundation | phase-1 | phase-2a | phase-2b | phase-3 | all.
  Each phase has a discrete bead that wires its driver
  (foundation: 42e.11, phase-1: upe.14, phase-2a: 63g.18, phase-2b:
  8vf.9, phase-3: hrw.27 + hrw.28 + hrw.29). The xtask delegates to
  these and produces a unified summary.
- `cargo xtask logs validate <events.jsonl>` — validates an event
  stream against tests/logging/expected_event_shape.schema.json
  (committed by 42e.10). Useful both in CI and locally when an
  implementer is debugging a captured run.
- `cargo xtask redaction fuzz [--seed <hex>] [--iterations N]` — runs
  the secret-redaction fuzz suite (bsf.2). Default seed deterministic;
  CI nightly job runs with a different seed to widen coverage. On any
  leak, prints the offending pattern + the destination it leaked to
  (events.jsonl line, audit.log line, manifest path, …).
- `cargo xtask graph beads` — produces a CI-friendly summary of the
  bead graph (priority distribution, ready set, blocked count,
  cycles=0). Mirrors `br stats --json` but augments with graph
  metrics (longest dependency chain, fan-in for each epic audit
  bead). Used by the CI summary page (42e.3 acceptance #5).
- `cargo xtask docs check` — verifies AGENTS.md / README.md / docs/PRD.md
  cross-references; runs the same markdown-link-check as CI.
- `cargo xtask snapshot review` — interactive snapshot review (insta
  review wrapper). Works locally only; CI uses `--accept` workflow
  via maintainer commits.

Acceptance additions (extend the original list):
5. `cargo xtask e2e all` returns 0 only if every phase driver passes.
   Output is a per-phase PASS/FAIL summary; failures dump the
   captured events.jsonl + stderr per phase as artifacts.
6. `cargo xtask redaction fuzz` is reproducible from its seed; CI
   uploads the captured stream + seed on failure so a maintainer can
   replay locally.
7. `cargo xtask logs validate` exits non-zero on any schema violation
   and prints the JSON Pointer of the offending field. Also useful
   manually: a developer can capture stderr from any command, save it
   as events.jsonl, and run validate.
8. xtask itself uses the 42e.10 logging facade for its own progress
   messages — no println!/eprintln! (matches 42e.10's banned-macros
   rule). Verified by the same fitness test.

title: ADR scaffold — docs/adr/0000-template.md + README + index
description:
The Architecture Decision Record process is a load-bearing convention in
this repo: any new architectural choice must be recorded as an ADR before
it becomes load-bearing in code (AGENTS.md §7.1).

PRD refs: §19.4 (repo/contributor documentation), §24 (decisions table),
AGENTS.md §7.1, §9.3.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `docs/adr/0000-template.md` defines the ADR template with sections:
   Status (Proposed/Accepted/Superseded by NNNN), Context, Decision,
   Alternatives considered, Consequences, References.
2. `docs/adr/README.md` documents the process: numbering scheme, status
   lifecycle (Proposed → Accepted → optionally Superseded), how to link
   from PRD, and how to record supersession.
3. ADR index in `docs/adr/README.md` is kept in sync (alphabetized or
   numerically ordered list with one-line summaries).
4. Initial seed ADRs that capture decisions already in PRD §24 (each as
   a separate ADR) — record at minimum:
   - ADR: Frontend package manager = pnpm with committed pnpm-lock.yaml.
   - ADR: YAML parser primary = serde-saphyr; fallback = marked-yaml.
   - ADR: YAML authoring subset constrained per PRD §10.2.
   - ADR: Template renderer = restricted know-now-minijinja-v1 profile.
   - ADR: Trunk-based development on main, no feature branches, no PRs
     (AGENTS.md §7.6).
   - ADR: BMAD code-review gate by a different agent via mcp-agent-mail
     (AGENTS.md §7.8).

Do NOT:
- Treat ADRs as a place to re-state the PRD. ADRs explain *why* a choice
  was made; the PRD records *what* the choice is. Cross-link both ways.
- Backfill ADRs for implementation details — only architectural choices
  (AGENTS.md §7.1).

title: Cross-platform CI matrix — Linux/macOS/Windows incl. WSL paths
description:
Promote the CI test matrix to cover all three supported platforms early so
that path-handling and line-ending bugs surface on commit, not on release.

PRD refs: NFR-PO1, NFR-PO2, NFR-PO3, §17.7, §20.1.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Rust tests run on Linux, macOS, and Windows (native, not WSL).
   Linux is the primary platform; macOS + Windows must pass for any
   merge.
2. Path-handling tests cover spaces, Unicode (NFC + NFD if practical),
   emoji-in-path (where the platform allows), and platform separators
   (NFR-PO2). Tests live in know_now_writer + know_now_metadata
   (mirrored under tests/portability/ for the integration path).
3. Line-ending tests verify generated output uses stable line endings
   (PRD §11.7, NFR-PO3) — typically LF in generated files, even on
   Windows. Hexdump assertion under tests/portability/line_endings.rs.
4. Determinism cross-platform check: run a Phase 1 fixture on each
   platform and assert byte-identical artifacts for everything except
   .knownow/ (NFR-R1). This becomes a stronger gate as more generators
   land.
5. UTF-8 encoding assertion: generated files have no BOM (NFR-PO3) —
   tests/portability/utf8_encoding.rs scans the writer-emitted byte
   stream for U+FEFF and fails if present.
6. Non-ASCII identifier byte-sequence assertion: a fixture with
   non-ASCII entity / column / source names produces byte-identical
   output across all three OSes (NFC normalization preserved).
7. WSL is not a separate platform target; document that Windows native
   coverage is what matters and that WSL is best-effort.

Do NOT:
- Special-case Windows by forking code paths. If a feature can't be
  implemented portably, raise it as a blocking ADR.
- Use platform-specific path separators in generated content.
close_reason: Completed — 4 portability tests + docs + CI matrix already covers 3 platforms

title: Acceptance Criteria
description: - One

title: Epic — Phase 2A: Rust CLI MVP
description:
Phase 2A delivers the smallest useful Rust CLI and proves safe local
generation without requiring the dashboard. After Phase 2A a real consulting
engagement should be able to run the loop:

  know-now init --demo
  know-now check
  know-now generate --locked

…and get deterministic PostgreSQL DDL + Markdown documentation written
atomically with a manifest, lockfile, and ownership markers.

PRD refs: §6.2 (Phase 2A scope), §22 build sequence #7-#19, §23.2 (exit criteria).

Included CLI commands (PRD §12.1):
  init, validate, check, schema, generate, lock update, lock check,
  id check, id suggest, id backfill --dry-run, examples list,
  config inspect, version, version --capabilities

Excluded (PRD §6.2):
- Visual editing, dashboard, SaaS/hosted collaboration, team auth,
  source DB introspection, CSV/Excel import, NL metadata input,
  Data Vault, MDM, metrics layer, dbt generation, Mermaid generation,
  third-party policy packs, declarative template packs.

Exit criteria (PRD §23.2):
- Demo project initializes, validates, checks, generates PG DDL + docs.
- Atomic write/promote is production-ready.
- Manifest + lockfile behavior stable enough for CI.
- Diagnostics include file, line, column, YAML path, error code, object ID,
  suggested fix where possible.
- Output deterministic excluding explicit volatile state under .knownow/.
- JSON Schema exported and usable in VS Code.
- metadata/ and custom/ untouched by validation/generation.
- Performance targets met on 10-entity and 100-entity fixtures (NFR-P3..P5).
- ≥1 real consulting-style fixture modeled successfully.

Phase gate: built-in default policy profile only (META-010, PRD §14.2);
custom packs come in Phase 3.

## Success Criteria

Close this epic only when its child beads prove the described scope, every phase or NFR gate is linked to evidence, and remaining follow-up work is explicitly deferred into separate beads.

title: CLI entrypoint + clap-style command structure
description:
The CLI is the primary product surface in Phase 2A. Build the clap-based
command tree with stable subcommand names, unified standard flags, and
text/JSON/SARIF output renderers shared with the diagnostics layer.

PRD refs: §8.1 (clap-style command model), §12.1 (Phase 2A command list),
§12.2 (standard flags), §22 build sequence #11.

Phase 2A subcommand surface (PRD §12.1):
  init, validate, check, schema, generate, lock {update,check},
  id {check, suggest, backfill --dry-run}, examples list, config inspect,
  version, version --capabilities

Standard flags on every relevant command (PRD §12.2):
  --format text|json|sarif|quiet
  --verbose
  --debug
  --config <path>
  --project <path>
  --no-color

Generation-specific flags (PRD §12.2):
  --dry-run
  --target ddl|dbt|quality|docs|diagrams|review|fixtures|all
  --strict
  --fail-on-warnings
  --locked
  --no-cache
  --changed
  --prune stale|none
  --accept-generated-overwrite

## Acceptance Criteria
1. know_now_cli is the only [bin] crate; the rest are libraries.
2. Subcommand names match the PRD spelling exactly.
3. --help output matches CLI-003 acceptance: description, usage,
   options, examples per command.
4. --format json emits stable JSON (versioned envelope) for every
   command that produces structured output (CLI-005).
5. Text output is the human default; --format quiet emits only errors.
6. Exit codes (CLI-006): 0 success, 1 validation error, 2 usage error.
7. Cold-start <500 ms (NFR-P1) on the demo project; --help <200 ms
   (NFR-P2).
8. Subcommand registration uses an inventory pattern so later phases can
   add subcommands without modifying the entrypoint (NFR-SC2).

Do NOT:
- Reach into generator crates from the CLI; everything goes through
  know_now_core.
- Add subcommands not in the Phase 2A list (PRD §12.1) — Phase 2B/3
  commands are scoped to their own beads.
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; CLI ↔ logging facade integration):

Every subcommand emits, via the 42e.10 logging facade, exactly:

1. `command_start` event:
   { stage: \"command_start\", command, run_id, project_root_hash,
     engine_version, args_redacted, format, locked, dry_run, started_at }
   - args_redacted: all flag values that match the redaction pattern
     catalog are replaced with {redacted: true}.
   - project_root_hash: SHA-256 of the canonical absolute path; never
     the raw path (NFR-S18 / NFR-R13).

2. `command_complete` event on success:
   { stage: \"command_complete\", command, run_id, duration_ms,
     result: \"success\", exit_code: 0, artifacts_count?,
     warnings_count? }

3. `command_error` event on failure:
   { stage: \"command_error\", command, run_id, duration_ms,
     result: \"failure\", exit_code, error_code, severity }

Subcommand registry hook:
- The clap subcommand inventory pattern (acceptance #8) exposes a
  trait Cli Subcommand { fn run(&self, ctx: &CommandContext) -> Result… }
  where CommandContext owns the configured tracing subscriber and a
  RunId. The entrypoint wraps each invocation in a span automatically;
  individual subcommand implementors do NOT manually emit
  command_start / command_complete / command_error.
- This is the hook that hrw.24 (audit log) and bsf.2 (redaction) rely
  on so that any subcommand added later participates without
  per-command boilerplate.

Acceptance additions (extend the original list):
9. CLI fitness test enumerates every registered subcommand and
   verifies, by capturing tracing events with the test subscriber
   from 42e.10, that exactly one command_start and exactly one
   terminal event (command_complete OR command_error) appear per
   invocation.
10. JSON envelope schema versioned via constant
    know_now_cli::JSON_ENVELOPE_VERSION; snapshot tests for the
    envelope on every command produce stable output (no timestamps in
    the envelope; version + result + payload only).
11. Exit-code contract test: a property test that drives every
    subcommand with a fixture chosen to produce success / validation
    error / usage error and asserts the exit code matches CLI-006
    (0/1/2). New subcommands added later must register an expected-
    exit-code matrix entry; missing entries break the test.
12. --debug + --format json captures the stage timings (NFR-P12) for
    every subcommand. Snapshot the field NAMES (not values) for each
    command's expected stage stream under tests/cli/expected_stages/.

title: know-now.lock — schema, resolution, lock check/update, --locked
description:
The reproducibility lockfile that pins resolved versions for engine,
generator contracts, policy packs, template packs, target compatibility
profiles, and resolved semantic type mappings. Phase 2A delivers schema
+ resolution + lock check + lock update + --locked enforcement; template-
pack and external-toolchain entries land in Phase 3.

PRD refs: §9.5 (reproducibility lockfile), LIFE-008, NFR-S10, §22 build
sequence #15.

Lockfile contents (PRD §9.5):
- lockfile_schema_version
- engine_version
- metadata_schema_version
- generator_contract_version
- built-in generator versions
- policy.pack/version/hash
- template packs / renderer profiles (Phase 3)
- target compatibility profiles
- resolved semantic type mappings
- approved registry source hashes (Phase 3)
- optional external toolchain constraints (NOT local executable paths)

Subcommands:
  know-now lock update                 explicit refresh; require user
                                       to review the diff
  know-now lock update --accept-contract-upgrade
                                       acknowledge breaking generator
                                       contract upgrade
  know-now lock check                  verify resolved versions match
                                       lockfile (CI-suitable)
  know-now lock check --format json    machine-readable for CI policy
                                       enforcement

Generation flag:
  know-now generate --locked           fail when resolved versions
                                       differ; do NOT allow floating
                                       generator/policy/template/
                                       contract versions

Hard rules (PRD §9.5):
- CI examples use --locked.
- Lockfile excludes local paths, timestamps, usernames,
  environment-specific executable locations.
- Lockfile updates are explicit and reviewable (not implicit on every
  generate).
- Manifest records lockfile hash (already in §8.11).
- LIFE-008 acceptance #5: contract upgrades are classified as
  compatible/warning/breaking.
- LIFE-008 acceptance #6: CI can fail specifically on lockfile drift.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. lock update creates know-now.lock idempotently.
2. lock check exits non-zero when versions drift from lockfile.
3. --locked mode in generate refuses to run with version mismatch.
4. Schema is versioned and migration-safe; tests cover migration
   between schema versions.
5. Lockfile JSON is canonical pretty-printed (deterministic).

Do NOT:
- Resolve from a network registry in Phase 2A. All packs are built-in
  (default policy) or local in this phase.
- Write user paths into the lockfile.
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; lockfile schema migration + breaking-change tests):

Schema migration discipline (anchors PRD §9.5: \"Lockfile schema
migrations are deterministic and reported in release notes\"):

Error code catalog for lock workflow (used by CI policy enforcement
in lock check --format json):
- LOCK-SCHEMA-001: lockfile_schema_version mismatch — the engine
  recognizes a newer / older schema than the lockfile. Refuse to act;
  emit remediation that points at the migration command (when one
  exists) or at the documented hand-edit path.
- LOCK-CONTRACT-002: generator_contract_version bump is breaking;
  --accept-contract-upgrade is required and was not passed.
- LOCK-STALE-003: --locked mode + resolved versions differ from
  lockfile (any field — engine, generator, policy, template,
  renderer, contract). Print the field-by-field diff with both sides;
  exit non-zero.
- LOCK-MISSING-004: --locked mode + lockfile absent.
- LOCK-CORRUPT-005: lockfile present but parse-fails or hash check
  fails.
- LOCK-UNKNOWN-006: lockfile contains a field the current engine
  does not recognize (forward-compat tolerance: emit a warning, do
  not fail, unless --strict-lockfile is set).

Migration tooling:
- `know-now lock update` performs the standard refresh; for breaking
  generator-contract changes, requires `--accept-contract-upgrade`.
- The lockfile schema migration is its own pure function from
  (old_lockfile, target_schema_version) to (new_lockfile,
  migration_diff). Deterministic given the inputs.
- Migration diff is human-readable + JSON-serializable; release
  notes consume it (PRD §9.5).
- Schema version constants live in
  know_now_lock::SCHEMA_VERSIONS as an enum so tests + migration
  code share the source of truth.

Tests:
1. Snapshot of every supported lockfile schema version under
   fixtures/lockfile/<version>/ (initially just \"1.0\"; future
   versions land alongside their migration tests). Each fixture
   carries: a known-good lockfile.json, the manifest it pairs with,
   and the metadata input that produced it.
2. Migration tests: for each version pair (N → N+1), assert
   migration is deterministic (run twice, compare byte-for-byte) +
   produces the documented diff.
3. CI gate: `lock check --format json` against a stale lockfile must
   fail with LOCK-STALE-003 + the field-by-field diff. Exit code is
   tested.
4. Property test: `lock update` is idempotent on identical inputs
   (running it twice produces byte-identical lockfile content;
   anchors NFR-R1).
5. Unknown-field test: a lockfile carrying a future-version field
   produces LOCK-UNKNOWN-006 as a warning by default; with
   --strict-lockfile set, the warning becomes an error.
6. Hash-verification test: tamper any tracked field's hash; expect
   LOCK-CORRUPT-005.

Acceptance additions (extend the original list):
4. Error codes above are implemented + tested with a fixture per
   code.
5. Schema versions enumerated in
   know_now_lock::SCHEMA_VERSIONS; fitness test asserts every
   discriminant has a matching fixture + a migration path.
6. `lock check --format json` envelope includes a top-level
   `lock_check_schema_version` (parallel to doctor / audit-log
   schemas in hrw.6 / hrw.24).
7. CI integration: `lock check --format json --locked` runs against
   the demo fixture on every PR; output is captured and asserted to
   match the expected envelope shape (no drift).

title: Artifact ownership markers + manual-edit detection
description:
Generated files carry a machine-readable ownership header where the
target format supports comments. Before replacing an existing generated
file, the writer compares its content hash to the previous manifest's
recorded hash; mismatch blocks promotion.

PRD refs: §11.8 (ownership markers + manual-edit detection), GEN-013,
AGENTS.md §4.4 (no self-hash embedding), §22 build sequence #16.

Header format (PRD §11.8) — adapt comment syntax per target:
  Generated by know-now.
  Artifact ID: <id>
  Generator: <name>
  Input hash: sha256:<hash>
  Manifest path: generated/manifest.json
  Do not edit directly unless you intend to fork this artifact.

Hashing rules (PRD §11.8):
- Manifest records artifact hashes.
- Generated files do NOT embed their own full-file content hash.
- If a format requires embedded hashes, the hash mode must explicitly
  exclude the hash field itself.
- Manual edit detection uses the previous manifest, not only the file
  header.

Edit detection behavior:
- Default: block promotion and report the edited file.
- --accept-generated-overwrite: replace after explicit confirmation.
- --adopt-generated-edits: future Phase 3+ command to convert safe
  changes back into metadata where possible.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. SQL, Markdown, YAML, JSON outputs all carry the header in their
   appropriate comment syntax.
2. Manual edit test: change one byte in generated/ddl/postgres/
   schema.sql; next generate fails with WRITER-EDIT-DETECTED naming
   the file.
3. --accept-generated-overwrite proceeds and records a warning in the
   manifest.
4. Deletion of a previously generated file is reported as
   WRITER-MISSING-PRIOR (warning) and the file is recreated.
5. Tests: header parsing roundtrip, edit detection, accept-overwrite.

Do NOT:
- Embed the file's own content hash inside the file header (AGENTS.md
  §8 hard don't).
- Use timestamps in the header (NFR-R13).

title: Stale artifact detection + --prune none|stale
description:
Generated artifacts that existed in the previous manifest but are absent
from the new generation plan are stale. Surface them; allow safe
deletion only of files recorded in the previous manifest.

PRD refs: §9.4 (stale artifact behavior), NFR-R12.

Behavior:
- Compare new ArtifactDescriptors against previous manifest entries.
- Stale = path in previous manifest, not in new plan.
- --prune none (default for solo profiles): list stale artifacts as
  warnings; do not delete.
- --prune stale: delete stale artifacts (only files recorded in the
  previous manifest; never untracked files — PRD §9.3).
- Untracked files in generated/ are reported as WRITER-UNTRACKED
  (warning) and never deleted.

Defaults (PRD §9.4):
- minimal profile: --prune none.
- consultant-postgres-dbt: --prune none.
- governed-team: --prune stale (warn first).
- demo: --prune stale (acceptable churn).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Stale detection has a positive test (rename an entity, re-generate,
   verify old entity's docs are reported stale).
2. --prune stale only deletes manifest-tracked files (negative test:
   add an untracked file, --prune stale, file remains).
3. CI examples in docs make pruning behavior explicit.

title: validate command — full discovery + parse + semantic + policy
description:
The know-now validate command runs discovery + parsing + schema +
semantic + policy validation and reports diagnostics. No artifact
writes.

PRD refs: §12 (CLI), META-003, META-004, META-006, META-010, CLI-006,
CLI-007.

Pipeline (subset of PRD §8.5 #1-#7):
1. Discovery (S_P2A_DISCOVERY).
2. Parsing + YAML subset enforcement (S_P1_PARSER).
3. Schema validation via JSON Schema export.
4. Semantic validation -> ProjectGraph.
5. Built-in policy validation (S_P2A_POLICY).
6. Default resolution (no mutation; produces a resolved view used by
   downstream stages but discarded after diagnostics).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Exit code 0 on success, 1 on validation errors (CLI-006).
2. --format text|json|sarif|quiet supported.
3. metadata/ and custom/ unmodified after validate (META-006 acceptance).
4. validate --format json suitable for CI annotations.
5. validate --format sarif valid against SARIF 2.1.0.
6. Performance: 100 entities <2s (NFR-P3).

Do NOT:
- Run generation or write any file under generated/ from validate.
- Pull policy packs from disk (Phase 3).

title: check command — recommended local + CI verification
description:
know-now check is the *recommended* local and CI verification command.
It runs the full pipeline through dry-run artifact generation and
configured artifact validation but writes no files unless explicitly
requested.

PRD refs: §12.5 (check), CLI-010, §22 build sequence #17.

Pipeline (PRD §12.5):
1. Metadata discovery.
2. YAML parsing.
3. Schema validation.
4. Semantic validation.
5. Policy validation.
6. Lockfile consistency check (when --locked).
7. Capability check.
8. Generation planning.
9. Dry-run artifact generation (artifacts in memory; nothing written).
10. Configured artifact validation (SQL parser, optional dbt validation
    in Phase 2B).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. check writes no generated artifacts unless --target=ddl --write
   (or similar) is explicitly passed.
2. --format text|json|sarif|quiet supported.
3. CI examples use know-now check --format json --locked.
4. Optional code-scanning examples use --format sarif --locked.
5. Exit 0 only when project is safe to generate.
6. check --locked enforces lockfile consistency (LIFE-008).

Do NOT:
- Add artifact-writing side effects.
- Make check slower than 2x validate; it must be the everyday
  pre-commit / pre-push command.

title: generate command — all flags + planning + dry-run
description:
The generate command is the workhorse: discovery → parse → graph →
contract → plan → generate → validate → write → manifest. Phase 2A
ships the full flag set even though only ddl/docs targets exist
(dbt/quality/diagrams/fixtures land in 2B).

PRD refs: §12.1, §12.2 (generation flags), §8.5 (generation pipeline),
§8.8 (--migration-safe), GEN-006, GEN-009, GEN-010, GEN-011, §22 build
sequence #12.

Flags (PRD §12.2):
  --dry-run                       no writes; print plan + summary
  --target ddl|dbt|quality|docs|diagrams|review|fixtures|all  (default
                                  uses configured targets from
                                  know-now.yml)
  --strict                        treat warnings as errors
  --fail-on-warnings              fail on any warning
  --locked                        require lockfile consistency
  --no-cache                      bypass content-addressed cache
                                  (cache itself is Phase 3)
  --changed                       incremental (Phase 3)
  --prune stale|none              stale handling (S_P2A_STALE)
  --accept-generated-overwrite    bypass manual-edit detection
  --migration-safe                Phase 3 (require stable IDs +
                                  migration stub generation; PRD §8.8).
                                  In Phase 2A this flag is parsed and
                                  rejected with a clear "Phase 3
                                  feature; track via S_P3_DIFF /
                                  S_P3_MIGRATIONS" message and exit
                                  code 2 (CLI-006 usage).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. generate --dry-run writes no files (GEN-009 acceptance #1).
2. generate (no flags) uses configured targets and produces all
   artifacts atomically (GEN-006 acceptance).
3. Manifest written after success (GEN-010).
4. Failure preserves prior generated/ (GEN-011 acceptance #3).
5. --target accepts comma-separated lists (e.g., --target=ddl,docs).
6. --target=all is a deterministic alias for the union of supported
   targets in this build.
7. --target=changed (Phase 3) and --target=fixtures (Phase 2B) error
   in Phase 2A with a clear "phase 2B/3 feature" message and exit 2.
8. --migration-safe (Phase 3) errors in Phase 2A with the same
   phase-aware message and exit 2; Phase 3 rewires this flag through
   S_P3_DIFF + S_P3_MIGRATIONS without changing the CLI surface.
9. Performance budgets: 10 entities <5s, 100 entities <60s
   (NFR-P4, P5).
10. The flag set + phase-aware error messages are snapshot-tested so
    accidental promotion of a Phase 3 flag in 2A surfaces in code
    review.

Do NOT:
- Allow concurrent generate runs in the same project (writer lock
  enforces).
- Read .knownow/cache/ in Phase 2A (introduce in Phase 3).
- Silently accept Phase 3 flags in Phase 2A — fail explicit + early.

title: examples list + config inspect + version + version --capabilities
description:
Three small but load-bearing CLI utilities. Bundled here because each
is a thin layer over data the rest of Phase 2A already produces.

PRD refs: §12.1, §12.3 (examples list, config inspect), §8.4 (version
--capabilities), CLI-011.

  know-now examples list             list bundled offline examples
                                     and brief descriptions
  know-now config inspect            show resolved config, profile,
                                     policy, target versions,
                                     generator capabilities, lockfile
                                     state, effective defaults
  know-now version                   engine version + build info
  know-now version --capabilities    structured generator capability
                                     report (uses §8.4 registry)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. examples list works fully offline (NFR-CLI-007).
2. config inspect modifies no files (CLI-011 acceptance #3).
3. config inspect --format json is suitable for CI / scripting.
4. version --format json includes engine version, metadata schema
   version, generator contract version, lockfile schema version.
5. version --capabilities prints the capability registry: each
   built-in generator with name, version, accepted contract versions,
   target dialects/versions, supported types, validation gates,
   known unsupported constructs, experimental flag.

Do NOT:
- Make any of these contact the network (NFR-CLI-007).

title: CI integration examples — GitHub Actions + GitLab CI templates
description:
Ship runnable CI templates so that Phase 2A users can wire know-now into
their pipelines without writing YAML from scratch.

PRD refs: §6.2 (Phase 2A includes CI integration examples), §12.5
(CI examples use check --format json --locked), CLI-006.

Templates to ship under examples/ci/:
- examples/ci/github-actions/know-now.yml  PR + main; uses check
  --format json --locked; pins know-now via cargo binstall.
- examples/ci/gitlab-ci/.gitlab-ci.yml      same shape for GitLab.
- examples/ci/circleci/config.yml           same for CircleCI.
- examples/ci/buildkite/pipeline.yml        same for Buildkite.

Each template:
- Pins the know-now version via the lockfile or a workflow input.
- Caches Rust toolchain only when the repo includes know-now-as-source.
- Annotates PR with diagnostics where the platform supports JSON
  annotations.
- Uses --format sarif as an optional code-scanning job.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Each template has a README that explains required permissions and
   trade-offs.
2. CI in this repo's CI sanity-checks each template renders to valid
   YAML.
3. Templates do not embed secrets or org-specific paths.

Do NOT:
- Send telemetry from any template.
- Recommend `cargo install` over the binary install path in the
  primary template — the binary path is faster and matches NFR-SS5.

title: Snapshot, integration, compatibility-fixture, property tests for Phase 2A
description:
The full Phase 2A test suite. Without this, the exit criteria are
unprovable. Stage taxonomy is canonical in 42e.10 (docs/dev/logging.md);
span names referenced below are the Phase-2A-specific entries within
that taxonomy.

PRD refs: §20.1 (CI checks — snapshot, property, mutation, fixture-diff),
§20.2 (compatibility fixtures), NFR-R2, NFR-R3, NFR-R6, NFR-R14, §22 build
sequence #18, §23.2 (exit), 42e.10 (structured logging facade).

Test layers:
1. Snapshot tests (insta or equivalent) for every generator output:
   - know_now_gen_postgres
   - know_now_gen_docs
   - manifest writer
   - diagnostics text + JSON renderers
   - parser fixtures (already from Phase 1)
2. Integration tests:
   - End-to-end on the demo fixture: init -> validate -> check ->
     generate -> assert artifact set + manifest.
   - Atomic-failure preservation (S_P1_WRITER tasks).
   - Manual-edit detection.
3. Property-based tests (proptest):
   - Random valid metadata graphs round-trip parse -> graph -> emit
     -> reparse without loss.
   - Unknown reference fixtures generate the expected diagnostic.
   - Duplicate ID fixtures fail.
   - Stable ID matching is symmetric and transitive.
   - Path normalization on all OSes is consistent.
4. Compatibility fixtures (PRD §20.2 — Phase 2A subset):
   - minimal project
   - ecommerce demo
   - missing-ID project
   - rename-heavy project
   - large 100-entity project
   - documentation-quality-warning project
   For each release, CI produces a fixture diff summary when generated
   outputs change (CI gate owned by bsf.9 AC #5).
5. Mutation-style tests for default policy pack rules (PRD §20.1).

6. End-to-end shell driver with verbose JSON-log assertions
   (`cargo xtask e2e phase-2a`):
   - Runs the full pipeline against the demo + minimal + governed
     fixtures with --verbose --format json; redirects events to
     events.jsonl per scenario.
   - Asserts every Phase 2A pipeline span (PRD §8.5 #1-#18) appears
     with duration_ms recorded (NFR-P12), in stable order, with
     stage names from the 42e.10 taxonomy.
   - Asserts no log content leaks into generated/ or
     generated/manifest.json (NFR-R13 + fitness #4).
   - Asserts no secrets in events.jsonl, .knownow/audit.log, or
     support-bundle archives across all scenarios.
   - On failure, dumps events.jsonl, .knownow/staging/<run_id>/, and
     CLI stderr as CI artifacts.
   - Cross-OS coverage: same xtask runs on Linux, macOS, Windows
     (NFR-PO1, NFR-R1).

7. Logging snapshot:
   - events.jsonl shape (stage names + field presence — NEVER
     timestamps or duration values) is committed under
     tests/e2e/phase_2a/expected_log_shape/ as a JSON-Schema sketch
     so changes to the log taxonomy show up in code review.

8. Read-only-command metadata-stability sweep (NFR-R6):
   - For each command in {validate, check, schema, id check,
     id suggest, lock check, version, config inspect}: run on the
     demo + governed + missing-ID fixtures and assert metadata/ tree
     is byte-identical before/after (recursive hash + mtime
     unchanged).
   - Fixture: tests/nfr_reliability/metadata_stability/.
   - This AC absorbs the formerly-cross-cutting NFR-R6 work that
     bsf.3 had homeless. bsf.3 now references this AC.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Coverage >=90% overall, >=95% for know_now_metadata,
   know_now_codegen, know_now_writer (NFR-R2).
2. cargo xtask check runs the full Phase 2A test suite green on the
   reference machine.
3. cargo xtask e2e phase-2a green on Linux + macOS + Windows
   (NFR-PO1, NFR-R1).
4. Logging assertions pass: every §8.5 stage span emitted, no log
   content leaks into deterministic artifacts, no secrets in logs.
5. Read-only-command metadata-stability sweep (layer 8) green —
   every read-only CLI command leaves metadata/ byte-identical
   (NFR-R6).

Do NOT:
- Use real timestamps or random IDs in snapshots — use
  SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH and a seeded PRNG (PRD §8.11).
- Hard-code OS-specific path separators in expected snapshots.
- Snapshot duration_ms values — they are volatile by design. Snapshot
  only stage names + ordering + presence of fields.
- Use println!/eprintln! in the test driver — go through the same
  tracing facade (42e.10) so the harness output is itself structured.

title: Phase 2A exit-criteria audit + AGENTS.md status update
description:
Closing bead for Phase 2A. Walk PRD §23.2 line by line and update
AGENTS.md §2 from "Phase 1 complete" to "Phase 2A complete; Phase 2B
active."

PRD refs: §23.2 (Phase 2A exit criteria), AGENTS.md §2.

Checklist:
- [ ] demo project initializes, validates, checks, generates successfully
- [ ] generated PostgreSQL DDL executes in CI (NFR-R4) — proved by 63g.7.4
- [ ] Markdown documentation generates correctly
- [ ] manifest records artifact hashes, metadata hash, lockfile hash
- [ ] know-now.lock supports reproducible generation
- [ ] no user-owned files are overwritten
- [ ] manually edited generated files are detected
- [ ] stale generated artifacts are detected
- [ ] CLI works on Linux, macOS, and Windows (NFR-PO1)
- [ ] JSON Schema is exported and usable in VS Code (META-008)
- [ ] performance targets met for 10-entity and 100-entity fixtures
       (NFR-P3, P4, P5) — proved by 63g.18 + bsf.1
- [ ] structured-logging facade emits the §17.1 stage spans with
       duration_ms via --verbose / --format json (NFR-P12) — proved by
       42e.10 + asserted by 63g.18 (cargo xtask e2e phase-2a)
- [ ] e2e shell driver passes on Linux + macOS + Windows
       (events.jsonl asserts + cross-OS check + secret-redaction smoke
       + no-log-leakage-into-generated/ — 63g.18)
- [ ] at least one real consulting-style fixture has been modeled
       successfully

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Each checklist item linked to a passing test or merged bead.
2. AGENTS.md §2 updated.
3. Tag a 0.2.0 (or equivalent) release per docs/dev/versioning.md.

title: init command + project profiles + --guided + git policy
description:
The know-now init command bootstraps a project with sensible defaults for
the user's role. Profile choice is a major UX decision — get it right.

PRD refs: §12.3 (initialization profiles), §9.7 (git policy), LIFE-001,
LIFE-002, §22 build sequence #7.

Subcommand surface (PRD §12.3):
  know-now init <name> --profile minimal
  know-now init <name> --profile consultant-postgres-dbt
  know-now init <name> --profile dbt-existing-stack
  know-now init <name> --profile governed-team
  know-now init <name> --profile demo
  know-now init <name> --guided
  know-now init --demo                  (alias for --profile demo)

Generated files (PRD §9.1, §12.3):
- know-now.yml — project config with profile-specific defaults.
- metadata/ tree (project.yml + profile-appropriate skeleton).
- generated/.gitkeep + .gitignore wiring per --generated-git-policy.
- custom/ tree (always created; never written to by engine).
- .knownow/ scaffolded.
- README.md inside the project explaining the layout.

Git policy flag (PRD §9.7):
  --generated-git-policy ignore|commit|ask
Defaults per profile:
  minimal=ignore, consultant-postgres-dbt=ask, dbt-existing-stack=ask,
  governed-team=commit, demo=commit.

--guided rules (PRD §12.3):
- A small number of local-only questions (project name, target DB, dbt
  validation mode, policy pack, governance level).
- Never sends telemetry or contacts external services.
- Writes the resulting know-now.yml; user can edit afterward.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. init --demo creates a working demo project that immediately validates
   + checks + generates without further input (LIFE-002 acceptance).
2. Each profile produces a know-now.yml that passes validate.
3. Generated config sets sensible defaults for: target_database,
   policy.pack, dbt validation mode (none for non-dbt profiles),
   --generated-git-policy.
4. init --demo creates a know-now.lock so subsequent --locked commands
   work (PRD §9.5: "init --demo creates a lockfile").
5. Tests cover all 5 profiles + --guided.
6. metadata/ and custom/ are created with .gitkeep files only — engine
   never writes other content to them in subsequent runs.

Do NOT:
- Send any network request from --guided.
- Use a profile name that isn't in the PRD §12.3 list. New profiles
  require a PRD update.

title: Metadata YAML discovery across multiple files in metadata/
description:
Auto-discover all metadata YAML files under metadata/, parse them into
ParsedMetadataDocument, and merge into a single AuthoringMetadata before
graph construction. Cross-file references are resolved at the graph
layer, not here.

PRD refs: §10.1 (metadata files auto-discovered), §22 build sequence #8,
META-001 (cross-file references resolve), META-006 (metadata/ not
rewritten).

Discovery rules:
- Recursively walk metadata/ for *.yml and *.yaml files.
- Each file must declare top-level `version:` (META-007).
- Single-document only (multi-doc rejected per §10.2).
- File ordering MUST be deterministic (sorted by relative path) so that
  identical inputs produce identical graphs.
- Symlinks under metadata/ are not followed.
- Files in subdirectories matching the §9.1 layout (domains/, modules/,
  entities/, sources/, relationships/, rules/, governance/, questions/)
  are still general — the *content* of each file determines what objects
  it carries; the directory is just organization.
- Hidden files (.*) are skipped.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Discovery is part of know_now_core::project_loader; deterministic
   file ordering is unit-tested.
2. Discovery emits per-file ParsedMetadataDocument with SourceSpanIndex
   and merges into a single AuthoringMetadata.
3. Conflicting top-level versions across files produce a clear
   META-VER-MISMATCH error (PRD §10.1 implicitly).
4. metadata/ is NOT modified by discovery (META-006 acceptance: hash
   files before/after).
5. 100-entity multi-file project completes parse + discovery in <2 s
   (NFR-P3).
6. Multi-file fixture (valid_multifile_project) covers ≥3 files
   referencing each other.

Do NOT:
- Apply policy defaults during discovery — that is a separate pipeline
  step (PRD §8.5 step 7).
- Re-format YAML during read.

title: Built-in default policy profile + policy validation engine
description:
Phase 2A ships a single built-in default policy pack (dc_standard 1.0)
plus the side-effect-free evaluation engine. Custom packs and drift come
in Phase 3 (PRD §14).

PRD refs: §14 (policy packs), §14.2 (default policy pack), §14.4 (engine
rules), META-010, §22 build sequence #11.

Built-in pack contents (dc_standard 1.0) — useful but not strict:
- Naming conventions: snake_case for entity names, attribute names,
  module names; lowercase ASCII identifiers (warn).
- Required attributes: warn when entity is missing primary-key
  attribute or when business_key is absent.
- Allowed semantic types: union of PRD §10.8 list (error on unknown).
- Allowed logical types: union of PRD §10.7 list (error on unknown).
- Default semantic type mappings (used during contract projection): see
  PRD §10.8 — identifier→PK candidate, email→VARCHAR(320), etc.
- Default dbt materializations (Phase 2B will use): staging→view,
  marts→table.
- Default quality rules: NOT NULL on required + UNIQUE on business_key
  attributes.
- Documentation conventions: warn when entity description is empty;
  warn when attribute description is missing on required attrs.
- Severity defaults: warning for everything except unknown semantic /
  logical type, which is error.

Engine rules (PRD §14.4, NFR-S22 spirit):
- Policy evaluation is side-effect-free. NEVER mutates AuthoringMetadata
  or ProjectGraph (fitness-test #9).
- Policy-provided defaults are applied through an explicit resolution
  step (a separate "default resolution" pipeline stage, PRD §8.5 #7)
  with each defaulted value carrying a trace pointing at the policy
  rule that supplied it.
- Manifest records policy.pack, policy.version, policy.hash (GEN-010).
- Unknown policy features fail validation in --locked mode unless
  explicitly allowed by an experimental profile (META-010 acceptance #6).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. dc_standard 1.0 ships embedded in the binary (no filesystem
   discovery needed for the default pack).
2. Each rule has a stable code (e.g., POL-NAM-001), severity, rationale
   text, and remediation example (PRD §14.7).
3. Findings carry source spans pointing at the offending object.
4. Mutation-style tests (PRD §20.1) verify the engine cannot mutate
   inputs.
5. Fitness test asserts know_now_policy does not export any &mut access
   to ProjectGraph or AuthoringMetadata.

Do NOT:
- Allow rule code execution. Phase 2A is declarative only (PRD §14.4:
  "External policy packs are data-only in Phase 2 and Phase 3").
- Read user-defined policy packs from disk in this bead — that is
  Phase 3 (PRD §6.4).

title: Stable object IDs — validation, suggest, backfill --dry-run
description:
Stable IDs power diffing, rename detection, traceability, and (Phase 3)
migration generation. Phase 2A ships validation, suggestion, and
dry-run backfill; --apply lands in Phase 3 (PRD §10.11 explicit
metadata-writing rules).

PRD refs: §8.8 (stable object IDs), §22 build sequence #9, META-009.

ID rules (PRD §8.8):
- Globally unique within a project (unless an explicit namespace rule
  says otherwise).
- Lowercase ASCII + digits + underscores.
- Stable prefixes: dom_, mod_, ent_, attr_, rel_, src_, rule_, pol_.
- Attribute IDs remain stable even if attribute is renamed/moved.
- Recommended in Phase 2A; required for migration-safe workflows
  (--migration-safe, Phase 3).

Subcommands:
  know-now id check                 list missing/required IDs (META-009)
  know-now id suggest               propose deterministic IDs to stdout
                                    (NEVER writes to disk; PRD §10.11
                                    distinguishes no-write suggestions
                                    from explicit write commands)
  know-now id backfill --dry-run    preview what id backfill --apply
                                    would write (--apply is Phase 3)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Duplicate IDs fail validation (META-009 acceptance #1).
2. Diffing layer (Phase 3) consumes these IDs as the primary match key.
3. Suggested IDs use the prefix convention and a deterministic hashing
   scheme over name + container path (so the same input produces the
   same suggestion).
4. id check --format json is suitable for CI (CLI-006).
5. id backfill --dry-run prints a unified-diff-style preview keyed by
   file path; tests verify the file content would be unchanged after a
   no-op preview.
6. --migration-safe mode (used in Phase 3) errors out when required IDs
   are missing.

Do NOT:
- Write to metadata/ in any Phase 2A id command (META-006).
- Rename existing IDs automatically. Renames are an explicit user action
  with full backups and previews (PRD §10.11).

title: JSON Schema export — schema command + IDE autocomplete
description:
Generate JSON Schema for the metadata authoring layer so editors and CI
can validate YAML before know-now even runs. Cross-reference enrichment
(e.g., entity-name autocomplete) is a curated additive layer where the
auto-derived schema does not suffice.

PRD refs: §8.1 (JSON Schema generated from Rust types), §10.2, META-008,
NFR-I3, §22 build sequence #11.

Subcommand:
  know-now schema                   write/print JSON Schema to stdout or
                                    --output <path>
  know-now schema --vscode <path>   write JSON Schema and VS Code
                                    settings.json fragment that wires
                                    YAML association

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Schema validates the demo fixture without errors.
2. Schema rejects invalid examples (use the fixtures from META-001..013
   negative tests).
3. Schema versions are pinned to metadata_schema_version
   (PRD §10 versioning).
4. VS Code wiring uses the redhat.vscode-yaml schema-association
   convention (no proprietary extension).
