title: Phase 3 server HTTP integration test suite (launch token, CSRF, CORS, CSP, write guards)
description:
Dedicated security-focused HTTP integration test suite for the local
Rust axum server. The server is the largest new attack surface introduced
in Phase 3 (PRD §13.2, NFR-S14..S16, NFR-S20, AGENTS.md §8 hard "don'ts").
A failure in this layer means an unauthenticated local attacker — or a
hostile JS context — could trigger a generation, exfiltrate metadata, or
escape the read-only default. We test every guard via real HTTP against
the running server, not by mocking around it. Stage taxonomy is canonical
in 42e.10 (docs/dev/logging.md); event-tagging conventions referenced
below (event.kind=security, request_id) are entries within that taxonomy.

PRD refs: §13.2 (local server), §13.3 (API behavior), §13.4 (API contract
rules), NFR-S14, NFR-S15, NFR-S16, NFR-S20, NFR-I8, NFR-M6, AGENTS.md §4.4
+ §8 (hard "don'ts"), §22 build sequence #31-#32.

What this bead covers (each item is a discrete, named test):

A. Network binding (NFR-S14, AGENTS.md §8):
   1. Default `know-now serve` binds 127.0.0.1; remote IP rejected at
      the socket layer.
   2. `know-now serve --host 0.0.0.0` prints the AGENTS.md §8 warning
      and continues, but rejects requests from non-loopback when
      --allow-generate is also passed without --allow-generate-on-network.
   3. IPv6 ::1 behaves like 127.0.0.1; non-loopback IPv6 follows the
      same rules as non-loopback IPv4.

B. Launch token + session (PRD §13.2, NFR-S15):
   1. Visiting /__open without a launch_token query param redirects to
      a help page (no API access).
   2. Valid launch_token issues a session cookie (HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax,
      Secure when behind TLS) and immediately invalidates the token in
      the server's launch-token store (single-use; reuse → 400).
   3. After session is established, query-string Bearer-style tokens
      are REJECTED on every API route (AGENTS.md §8 hard "don't").
   4. Session cookies survive a server restart only if the server
      maintains an on-disk session store with explicit user opt-in;
      default behavior is in-memory sessions cleared on restart.
   5. Session cookie tampering (modified value) is rejected with 401
      and the security event logged (42e.10 secrets-redacted).

C. CORS + origin checks (NFR-S15):
   1. CORS allowlist is exactly the served dashboard origin; any other
      Origin header receives 403 + CORS error.
   2. Preflight OPTIONS with a non-allowed origin → 403; with the
      allowed origin → expected Access-Control-Allow-* headers (no
      wildcards).
   3. Origin: null requests (file://, sandboxed iframes) are rejected.

D. Response headers (NFR-S20):
   1. Content-Security-Policy is present, restrictive, and includes
      `default-src 'self'` (no `unsafe-inline`, no `unsafe-eval`,
      no remote CDN domains).
   2. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff present on every response.
   3. X-Frame-Options: DENY present on every response.
   4. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin present.
   5. Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy + Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy where
      practical for the dashboard bundle.

E. Write-endpoint guards (NFR-S16, AGENTS.md §8):
   1. Without --allow-generate, POST /api/v1/generate returns 404
      (endpoint not registered) — not 403, not 405. The route does not
      exist when the flag is off.
   2. With --allow-generate, POST /api/v1/generate without a CSRF
      double-submit token → 403.
   3. With CSRF token but without the request-level confirmation token
      (form field) → 403.
   4. With both CSRF + confirmation tokens → 200 and the generation
      kicks off through the same artifact writer + lock as the CLI.
   5. With --host non-127.0.0.1 AND --allow-generate but WITHOUT
      --allow-generate-on-network → write endpoints disabled with a
      visible warning in the dashboard health view.

F. Architecture fitness for the server (PRD §17.6, NFR-M6):
   1. Fitness test: write endpoints unreachable when know_now_server is
      compiled without the `allow-generate` feature flag — even with a
      valid session (compile-time gate, not just runtime). This is the
      compile-time half of the guard whose runtime half is hrw.13's
      implementation.
   2. Fitness test: server has no path to know_now_metadata parser
      crates (NFR-S13 isolation).

G. API compatibility (NFR-I8 / NFR-M6):
   1. Dashboard startup hits /api/v1/version; if engine API contract
      version differs from the version the client was generated
      against, dashboard surfaces a clear warning (and tests assert
      the warning text + headers).
   2. /api/v1/version JSON shape pinned by snapshot test (any change
      requires a contract version bump per PRD §13.4).

H. Performance + observability:
   1. p95 entity list /api/v1/entities <200 ms on the 100-entity
      fixture (NFR-P9 absolute-budget integration check; bsf.1 owns
      the criterion benchmark + >20% regression CI gate).
   2. Every request emits a tracing span with a request_id (X-Request-Id
      response header); the test asserts presence + that secrets are
      not logged (42e.10 redaction).

I. Failure mode tests:
   1. Server panic in a handler returns 500 with a generic body; the
      span captures the error class but no internal type names leak
      to the client.
   2. Slow handler hits the configured timeout; the response is 504
      and the dashboard shows a useful retry hint.
   3. Server respects ctrl+c / SIGTERM; in-flight requests get up to
      a configured grace period.

Test harness shape:
- Tests live under crates/know_now_server/tests/security/ and run as
  `cargo test -p know_now_server --features test-server`.
- A separate `cargo xtask e2e phase-3-server` driver runs the full
  flow against a started server (using xshell or duct) so CI can
  also exercise it as an end-to-end shell scenario with structured
  logs.
- Reqwest + rustls is preferred for the test client (matches the
  dashboard's runtime expectations); cargo deny audits the choice.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Every item A.1 .. I.3 has a named, isolated test that fails loudly
   with a precise message (e.g., "expected 403 because origin not in
   allowlist; got 200").
2. Tests run on Linux + macOS + Windows in CI; Windows skips the
   symlink-related corner cases that don't apply.
3. Negative-control tests fail when the corresponding guard is
   deliberately disabled in a feature flag (proves the test catches the
   regression).
4. Security review checklist updated under docs/dev/security.md to
   cite each test by name.
5. Logging: every security event (token mismatch, CORS reject, write
   without confirmation) emits a tracing event tagged
   `event.kind=security` with redacted fields per 42e.10.

Do NOT:
- Mock the server. Every test runs against an actually-bound axum
  process (or in-process axum::Router under tower::ServiceExt::oneshot
  for the cheap tests; the security-critical tests use a real socket).
- Use 0.0.0.0 in CI tests — bind 127.0.0.1 + assert with a non-loopback
  source address simulated via interface trickery only when actually
  testing the warning/reject paths.
- Skip CSP tests when behind a proxy — the server emits CSP
  unconditionally.
- Log bearer tokens, launch tokens, or session cookies (NFR-S18 +
  42e.10 redaction). Tests assert absence of these in events.jsonl.

title: Phase 3 dashboard Playwright E2E + visual regression + cross-browser matrix
description: Phase 3 dashboard end-to-end test suite using Playwright (or equivalent) plus visual-regression coverage. Complements hrw.21 (axe-core accessibility audits) by exercising real user flows in a headless browser against the locally-bound server, with structured logging from both the dashboard and the server captured per scenario. Stage taxonomy is canonical in 42e.10 (docs/dev/logging.md); span/event names referenced below are the dashboard-specific entries within that taxonomy. PRD refs: §13 (dashboard scope), §13.5 (UX principles + WCAG 2.2 AA), §17.1 (NFR-P8 dashboard FCP), §17.2 (NFR-S7 stakeholder-facing escape), §17.5 (NFR-SC5 large-project navigation), §22 build sequence #33-#34, §23.4 (Phase 3 exit), AGENTS.md §6.2 (TS strict). User-flow scenarios (each scenario = one Playwright spec; each spec runs on Chromium + Firefox + WebKit): 1) Launch + session bootstrap (DASH default flow): - `know-now serve` is started by the test runner. - Browser navigates to the printed __open URL with launch_token. - Assert: redirect to / strips the token from the URL bar. - Assert: subsequent fetches use the cookie; query-string Bearer attempts (manually injected by the spec) are rejected. 2) Entity browsing (DASH-001, DASH-002, NFR-P8, NFR-P9): - On the demo fixture, the entity list renders <1 s after route change; client-side search by name, domain, governance label, attribute count works without a network round-trip when results are already in memory. - Detail page renders attributes, semantic types, business keys, source mappings, governance, open questions with stakeholder- friendly phrasing. - Assert: no raw YAML appears in stakeholder-facing views (PRD §13.5 design requirement). 3) Relationship graph + table fallback (DASH-003): - Graph renders within budget; pan/zoom + click + hover details work via keyboard equivalents. - The accessible table fallback is reachable via a single keyboard shortcut and exposes every relationship the graph shows. - Visual regression: graph layout snapshot is stable for the demo fixture across browsers (deterministic layout seed required). 4) Generation status + docs viewer + manifest viewer (DASH-004, DASH-005): - Last-generation panel reflects the current .knownow/last_generation .json; failed runs link to diagnostics. - Markdown viewer renders generated docs; Mermaid renders inline using the bundled renderer (no remote CDN). - Manifest viewer is JSON-tree navigable. 5) Artifact traceability + explain (DASH-006): - Click an entity → "what this generates" lists every generated artifact path that includes the entity in metadata_object_ids. - Click an artifact → metadata objects + policy rules + template inputs + validation gates + generator version. 6) Health/admin view (DASH-007, ADMIN-002): - Renders engine version, contract version, policy pack, target versions, template renderer profiles, issue counts, drift. - Renders security state (binding, token/session, write-mode flag). - Unsafe template-pack trust level visible (untrusted highlighted). 7) Review summary + open-question register + export (DASH-009): - Review summary lists changed-since-baseline items. - Open-question register filters by status, owner, due. - Marking items draft / needs-confirmation / confirmed / rejected / deferred persists to .knownow/review_state.json (assert via local filesystem read in the test). - Export to Markdown produces files under docs/exported/review_*/. - "Copy stakeholder-safe link" copies a URL without a token. 8) Write-mode flow (only when --allow-generate) — security-sensitive: - Trigger generate from the dashboard requires CSRF + the per-form confirmation token; without the token, the submit is blocked with a clear error. - On success, the events.jsonl from the server captures the generate.requested + generate.completed events with a stable request_id. 9) Error UX: - Backend returns 500 → dashboard surfaces a useful, non-technical error and a "see explain" link. - Backend returns 401 (session expired) → re-launch via the printed __open URL; no infinite loop. 10) Large-project navigability (NFR-SC5): - Stress fixture with 200 entities + 1000 relationships loads under the budget (FCP <1 s + interactive <3 s). - Search + filters + domain grouping + table fallback all stay responsive (no main-thread blocks >50 ms). 11) XSS payload smoke (NFR-S7 — absorbs former bsf.2 AC #3 fixture home for stakeholder-facing escape): - Inject HTML/JS payloads (e.g. ``, ``, `javascript:` URLs) into entity description, attribute description, source-mapping notes, open-question text, and entity name fields. - Assert: payload appears escaped in the entity list, entity detail, relationship-graph tooltip, docs viewer, and review export Markdown — never executed and never rendered raw. - Assert: Content-Security-Policy from hrw.27 D.1 blocks any inline-script execution that escapes the templating layer (negative-control test). Visual regression layer: - Per scenario, capture page screenshots at key checkpoints; baseline PNG committed under web/tests/visual/baselines/. - Diffs computed with pixelmatch; threshold 0.1% pixels. - Browser/OS-specific baselines kept separately to avoid font-rasterizer noise (Chromium-Linux, Firefox-Linux, WebKit-macOS, etc.). Logging integration (42e.10): - The test harness starts the server with --verbose --format json and captures events.jsonl per scenario. - The harness also captures the browser console + network log per scenario (via Playwright tracing) and uploads both as CI artifacts on failure. - The dashboard emits client-side structured events through a small log adapter; tests assert on event names + ordering (not on durations or run_ids). ## Acceptance Criteria 1. `pnpm --dir web test:e2e` runs every scenario green on the reference CI machine; `cargo xtask e2e phase-3-dashboard` is the canonical entry point. 2. Cross-browser matrix (Chromium / Firefox / WebKit) green; Windows limited to Chromium + Firefox (WebKit best-effort). 3. Visual regression baselines re-generated only via xtask `web tests:visual:bless --confirm`. 4. Logging: per-scenario events.jsonl (server) + Playwright trace.zip (browser) saved as CI artifacts. 5. WCAG 2.2 AA hrw.21 axe-core audits run alongside these scenarios; serious + critical violations fail the build. 6. NFR-SC5 stress scenario runs nightly (not on every PR) due to runtime; results recorded against a baseline. 7. Scenario 11 XSS smoke green on every PR (NFR-S7); fixture catalog referenced from docs/dev/security.md (bsf.2 index). Do NOT: - Use a remote browser farm by default — Playwright local browsers in CI runners. A remote farm is opt-in only via env config. - Snapshot Mermaid diagrams across all browsers as PNG (font rendering diverges); snapshot the SVG structure instead. - Embed any external CDN in test pages (PRD: bundle Mermaid into the dashboard). - Skip the cross-browser matrix to "save time" on PRs — fixed-cost CI budget upfront avoids release-time surprises.

title: Phase 3 comprehensive E2E + integration test suite (CLI + templates + policy + audit + admin)
description:
Phase 3 comprehensive end-to-end + integration test suite covering every
non-server, non-dashboard Phase 3 surface: diff/issues/migration stubs,
incremental cache, doctor/explain/support-bundle/policy/admin commands,
audit log, declarative template packs (restricted know-now-minijinja-v1
profile), custom policy packs + drift, approved-version catalog, review
export, id backfill --apply. Mirrors 63g.18 (Phase 2A) + 8vf.9 (Phase 2B)
for the rest of Phase 3 and integrates the structured logging facade
from 42e.10. Stage taxonomy is canonical in 42e.10 (docs/dev/logging.md);
span names referenced below are the Phase-3-specific entries within
that taxonomy.

PRD refs: §6.4 (Phase 3 scope), §8.6 (incremental + cache), §12.6-§12.8
(doctor/explain/support bundle), §14 (policy), §15.1 (templates),
§17.3 (NFR-R), §20.1 (CI checks), §20.2 (compatibility fixtures), §23.4
(Phase 3 exit criteria), AGENTS.md §4.4, §8.

Test layers:

1) Snapshot + integration tests (insta or equivalent), one per Phase 3
   crate / surface:
   - know_now_diff: stable-ID matching, change classification, --baseline
     last-generation / git:<ref> / manifest:<path>.
   - know_now_diff --impact: artifact impact list per changed object.
   - know_now_diff --scan-custom: exact ID, exact name, heuristic
     match in custom/ files.
   - know_now_diff --migration-safe: missing ID → fail-fast.
   - issues store under .knownow/issues.json: open / in_progress /
     resolved / snoozed transitions; deterministic IDs.
   - Migration SQL stubs: additive ADD COLUMN, confirmed RENAME,
     ambiguous TODO stub, destructive --accept-destructive gate,
     migration parses through sqlparser-rs.
   - Incremental cache: --changed regenerates only affected artifacts;
     cache-corruption hash-mismatch falls back to full rebuild;
     equivalence to full generation (NFR-R10).
   - id backfill --apply: backups under .knownow/backups/<ts>/;
     comments + formatting preserved through targeted YAML patcher
     (BOM-prefixed, leading-comments, mixed-indent fixtures).

2) Command integration tests (driven through know_now_cli):
   - doctor: text + JSON outputs; severity exit codes 0/1/2; no network;
     read-only.
   - explain: artifact path / metadata-object ID / error-code inputs;
     each variant produces expected fields; behaves correctly when
     manifest is stale.
   - support bundle: zip/tar archive layout; redaction unit tests
     (env vars, AWS-style keys, PEM blocks, $HOME paths, hostnames);
     metadata inclusion only with --include-metadata; --dry-run preview.
   - policy status: configured / locked / available / approved / drift
     classes (none/patch/minor/major/unknown/unapproved).
   - policy explain: rule + rationale + severity + remediation +
     org-specific text from pack manifest.
   - admin scan: walks tempdir of N projects, aggregates governance
     state to JSON; never modifies inspected projects.
   - admin catalog check: syntax + semver range validation.
   - review export: docs/exported/review_<date>/ tree; manifest hash +
     metadata hash + generation status included; never requires cloud.

3) Template-pack security suite (NFR-S22..S25 + EXT-004 + AGENTS.md
   §8 hard "don'ts"):
   - Manifest required → no manifest = template-load failure.
   - Output confined to declared subdirectory → escape attempts via
     `..`, absolute paths, dynamic include strings → reject.
   - Custom packs cannot register native MiniJinja functions / filters
     / tests / loaders → compile-time API gate test.
   - Strict undefined behavior → reference to undefined variable fails.
   - Fuel exhaustion → infinite loop fixture aborts with template
     fuel-exhaustion error.
   - Output-size, file-count, template-size, include-depth limits →
     each has a fixture that exceeds the limit and asserts rejection.
   - Static include cannot escape pack root → fixture fails predictably.
   - Diagnostics include template file/line/column/pack/profile/contract
     version.
   - Renderer output flows through artifact writer (path safety + atomic
     promote) — fitness test enforces (NFR-M5 #12-#16).
   - Trust level visible in doctor + manifest + dashboard health view.

4) Custom policy pack drift suite (PRD §14.6, ADMIN-005):
   - Valid pack: validate succeeds + policy.hash recorded.
   - Pack drift: change pack on disk, lockfile records old hash →
     `policy status` reports drift; `--locked check` exits non-zero.
   - Approved-version catalog: drift classes match PRD §14.6.

5) Audit log suite (NFR-S12, ADMIN-003):
   - Every CLI command appends an entry with command line + ts +
     project root + metadata hash + lockfile hash + engine version +
     result.
   - Secret patterns redacted (paste flags whose values look like
     secrets, env-var pass-through).
   - Log rotation at 10 MiB → archive under .knownow/audit/<datestamp>
     .log; rotation crash-safe.

6) Compatibility fixtures (PRD §20.2 — Phase 3 additions):
   - policy-drift project.
   - template-pack project.
   - template-pack strict-undefined failure.
   - template-pack dynamic-include rejection.
   - template-pack path-escape rejection.
   - template-pack fuel-exhaustion failure.
   - template-pack output-size failure.
   - template-pack untrusted-in-locked-mode failure.
   For each, fixture diff classification per §20.2 (CI gate owned by
   bsf.9 AC #5).

7) End-to-end shell driver (`cargo xtask e2e phase-3`):
   - Runs the full Phase 3 lifecycle on the demo + governed fixtures
     with --verbose --format json captured to events.jsonl.
   - Asserts a structured event per command per stage (42e.10
     taxonomy); no log content leaks into generated/ (NFR-R13).
   - Asserts no secrets in events.jsonl, .knownow/audit.log, or
     support bundle archives across all scenarios.
   - On failure, dumps events.jsonl, .knownow/staging/<run_id>/, and
     the entire .knownow/ directory tree as CI artifacts.

8) Property-based tests:
   - Stable-ID match is symmetric and transitive (LIFE-003).
   - Heuristic match below confidence threshold flags as ambiguous in
     --migration-safe mode.
   - Incremental == full (NFR-R10) over random valid graph
     mutations (add / rename / remove / move attribute).
   - Audit log line ordering is total + monotonic per process.

Coverage gates:
- Coverage >=90% overall, >=95% for know_now_diff, know_now_policy,
  know_now_templates, know_now_server (NFR-R2).
- Mutation-style tests for the new policy rules added in Phase 3.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. cargo xtask check + cargo xtask e2e phase-3 green on the reference
   CI machine.
2. Cross-OS CI green (NFR-PO1, NFR-R1).
3. Compatibility fixture diff suite green; release-notes-ready
   classification per PRD §20.2 (CI gate owned by bsf.9 AC #5).
4. Coverage gate met.
5. Logging: events.jsonl per scenario committed under
   tests/e2e/phase_3/expected_log_shape/ as JSON-Schema sketches
   (stage names + field presence — NEVER timestamps or durations).
6. Phase 3 exit audit (hrw.26) cites this bead's tests as the proof
   for each §23.4 checklist item.

Do NOT:
- Mock the template renderer. The point of these tests is to verify
  the real restricted know-now-minijinja-v1 profile blocks unsafe
  constructs.
- Use real wall-clock timestamps in deterministic snapshots —
  SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH for fixtures.
- Snapshot durations or run_ids. Snapshot stage names + field
  presence + ordering only.
- Write any test under metadata/ outside of id backfill --apply
  scenarios — no other Phase 3 command may mutate metadata/
  (PRD §10.11).

title: issues command — deprecation issue store under .knownow/
description:
Track deprecation issues that arise from breaking metadata changes.
Persistent under .knownow/issues.json so the same workflow survives
across runs and team handoff.

PRD refs: §12.1, LIFE-005, §22 build sequence #28.

Subcommand surface:
  know-now issues                  list unresolved issues
  know-now issues --status open|in_progress|resolved
  know-now issues resolve <id>     mark an issue resolved
  know-now issues snooze <id>      defer (user-readable reason)

Issue shape (LIFE-005):
- id (deterministic from change description hash so re-running is
  stable)
- affected_object (metadata object ID)
- change_type (compatible/breaking/destructive/ambiguous)
- description
- suggested_fix
- status (open/in_progress/resolved)
- created_at (volatile — under .knownow/)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Issues persist under .knownow/issues.json (LIFE-005 #2).
2. issues lists unresolved items (LIFE-005 #3).
3. JSON output suitable for dashboard ingestion.
4. issues resolve writes the change to .knownow/ atomically.

Do NOT:
- Persist issue state in metadata/. State belongs in .knownow/.
- Auto-resolve issues when a follow-up commit changes the affected
  object — the workflow requires explicit acknowledgement.

title: Docker Compose for full local demo stack (engine + dashboard + Postgres + dbt)
description:
Docker Compose stack for the full local know-now demo: engine + local
server + dashboard + PostgreSQL + optional dbt runner image, with the
demo project mounted in. Lets users run the entire local-first
experience without installing Rust, Node, dbt, or PG individually.

PRD refs: §18.3 (Docker Compose), NFR-I5 (Docker Compose supports full
local demo stack), §13 (dashboard + local API), §8.10 (dbt toolchain
adapter), §22 build sequence (Phase 3 release polish), AGENTS.md §4.4
(server defaults), §8 (no 0.0.0.0 default).

Stack composition:
- service: know-now-server  → image built from the workspace release
  binary; runs `know-now serve --bind 127.0.0.1:3827`. Inside the
  container, binds 127.0.0.1 + the container's published port. Compose
  publishes only on the host's loopback (127.0.0.1:3827) by default;
  --host 0.0.0.0 + --allow-generate-on-network is opt-in via a
  documented override file.
- service: know-now-dashboard → static asset image (or served by
  know-now-server directly; pick the simpler option). Bundled
  pnpm-lock.yaml + pinned packageManager.
- service: postgres            → official postgres:18 image (with 16/17
  variants documented); volume-mounted for stable local data.
- service: dbt (profile: dbt)  → optional, opt-in via
  `docker compose --profile dbt up`. Pinned dbt-core image + adapter
  (postgres). Compose passes the demo project's dbt config in.
- bind mount: ./demo → /workspace inside know-now-server; the demo
  metadata + custom + lockfile live here so users see + edit them on
  the host.
- volume: knownow-state → /workspace/.knownow inside the server so
  the audit log + run logs persist across restarts.

Configuration files:
- docker-compose.yml in the repo root with the read-only default.
- docker-compose.override.yml.example documenting the network-exposed
  override (--host 0.0.0.0 + --allow-generate-on-network) with a big
  warning comment matching PRD §13.2 + AGENTS.md §8.
- docker-compose.dbt.yml as the dbt-runner override.

CLI integration:
- README + docs/user/install.md document the Docker Compose path as
  one of the supported install routes (alongside cargo binstall +
  cargo install --locked + direct binary).
- `know-now examples list` includes a "docker-compose" example with a
  copy-paste `docker compose up` recipe.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `docker compose up` from a fresh checkout brings up the engine +
   dashboard + Postgres and the dashboard is reachable at
   http://127.0.0.1:3827 with the demo data loaded.
2. Default compose file binds the engine to host loopback only
   (NFR-S14, AGENTS.md §8). Network exposure requires the override
   file AND know-now serve flags.
3. `docker compose --profile dbt up` adds the dbt runner; the demo
   project compiles successfully through the dbt adapter (NFR-I7).
4. Images are reproducible: pinned by digest in the compose file
   when --locked equivalent is used (a separate compose-locked.yml).
5. Cross-platform: `docker compose up` works on Linux, macOS, and
   Windows (WSL2 + Docker Desktop). Document any platform-specific
   notes in docs/user/docker-compose.md.
6. Compose health checks: each service has a healthcheck stanza so
   `docker compose ps` shows healthy/unhealthy correctly.
7. Compose down preserves the user's metadata + .knownow/ via volumes;
   --volumes opt-in for full reset.
8. Logging: each service streams structured logs (server: tracing
   facade from 42e.10; dashboard: console + access log; postgres:
   default; dbt: compile output) so `docker compose logs` is the
   single pane for debugging.
9. Image size budget: server image <150 MiB compressed; dashboard
   image <50 MiB compressed (or zero if served by the server);
   postgres + dbt are upstream images.
10. CI smoke test: a CI job runs `docker compose up -d`, hits
    /api/v1/version, asserts a 200 + the expected engine version
    fields, then tears down. Runs on Linux only (Docker on macOS/
    Windows CI runners is too flaky as a hard gate; track best-effort).

Do NOT:
- Default the published port to 0.0.0.0:3827. Loopback only by default
  (AGENTS.md §8).
- Bake secrets into images (postgres password is set via env var with
  a clear "demo only — change for any real use" note).
- Push the images to a public registry without a release process; the
  Compose file uses local builds by default. Published images come
  later as part of the supply-chain track (bsf.8) and require ADR.
- Ship a default that requires dbt — dbt is opt-in via profile.
- Use `version: "3"` style legacy compose syntax; use the current
  compose specification format.

title: Migration SQL stubs — additive + confirmed-rename
description:
Generate migration SQL stubs alongside DDL when safe changes are
detected. Phase 3 supports additive (ADD COLUMN) + confirmed renames;
ambiguous and destructive changes require explicit confirmation and
produce manual stubs.

PRD refs: LIFE-004, §22 build sequence #26.

Output: generated/migrations/<timestamp_or_seq>__<change_id>.sql

Stub categories (LIFE-004):
- additive: ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN, CREATE TABLE for new entities,
  CREATE INDEX for new indexes.
- confirmed rename: ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN (stable IDs match).
- ambiguous: stub with -- TODO and explanation; user must edit.
- destructive: stub with -- DESTRUCTIVE marker and confirmation gate
  (require --accept-destructive flag at generation time).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Additive columns produce ADD COLUMN.
2. Confirmed renames produce rename statements (stable IDs match).
3. Ambiguous/destructive changes require explicit confirmation and may
   produce manual stubs.
4. Migration SQL parses successfully (parser-validation gate).
5. Migration SQL is applied to a temp DB in CI to verify it executes.

Do NOT:
- Auto-apply migrations. know-now generates SQL; the user runs it
  through a migration tool of their choice.
- Generate DROP COLUMN without explicit user opt-in (--accept-destructive).

title: Incremental generation + content-addressed cache
description:
Incremental generation regenerates only artifacts whose inputs changed,
backed by a content-addressed cache under .knownow/cache/.

PRD refs: §8.6, GEN-014, NFR-R10, §22 build sequence #27.

Cache contents (PRD §8.6):
- parsed metadata file hashes
- normalized graph hash
- generator input hashes
- artifact output hashes
- dependency edges metadata-object -> artifact
- prior generation plan summaries

CLI surface:
  know-now generate --changed
  know-now diff --affected-artifacts
  know-now generate --no-cache    (force full rebuild)

Rules (PRD §8.6):
- Incremental MUST produce identical final output to full generation
  (NFR-R10) — there is a property-test/snapshot equivalence gate in CI.
- Cache corruption detected by hash mismatch → discard, full rebuild.
- CI may disable cache for max reproducibility (--no-cache).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. generate --changed regenerates artifacts affected by changed
   metadata.
2. Incremental and full produce identical final output.
3. Cache corruption detected and recovered.
4. Incremental single-entity regeneration on 100-entity project <10 s
   (NFR-P6).

Do NOT:
- Cache in user-visible directories (cache lives under .knownow/cache/
  per PRD §9.1).
- Make --changed the default for generate; explicit opt-in to start.

title: doctor command — project + toolchain + config health
description:
The doctor command surfaces every "is this project healthy?" signal in
one place. Used by humans, support bundles (ADMIN-007), and CI.

PRD refs: §12.6, CLI-009, §22 build sequence #28.

Reported items (PRD §12.6):
- know-now version
- OS + architecture
- project root
- config file status
- lockfile status
- metadata schema version
- generator contract version
- metadata discovery summary (file count, total objects)
- policy pack status
- policy drift status (Phase 3 — needs admin scan / catalog)
- target database configuration
- dbt validation mode + availability (capability check via adapter)
- generator capability status (versioned matrix from registry)
- dashboard asset status
- unresolved issue count (.knownow/issues.json)
- last generation status (volatile run log)
- dependency / template warnings
- security warnings: network binding (Phase 3 server), unsafe config

## Acceptance Criteria
1. doctor reports project/toolchain/config health.
2. Supports text and JSON.
3. No network unless update checks are explicitly enabled.
4. Exit code: 0 healthy, 1 warnings, 2 errors. Document this contract
   so CI can pin to severity.
5. JSON output is consumable by support bundle.

Do NOT:
- Phone home for version checks unless --check-updates is passed.
- Mutate any state; doctor is read-only (PRD §10.11 implicit list).
notes:
SUPPLEMENTAL SCOPE (gap-close round 2; doctor JSON envelope + downstream
schema integration):

JSON envelope shape (used by support bundle hrw.8 + dashboard health
view hrw.19 + admin scan hrw.10):

{
  \"doctor_schema_version\": \"1.0\",
  \"engine\": { \"version\": \"…\", \"os\": \"…\", \"arch\": \"…\" },
  \"project\": { \"root_hash\": \"sha256:…\", \"config_present\": true,
                 \"metadata_files_count\": N, \"objects_count\": N },
  \"lockfile\": { \"present\": bool, \"valid\": bool, \"hash\": \"…\",
                  \"schema_version\": \"…\" },
  \"contracts\": { \"metadata_schema_version\": \"…\",
                   \"generator_contract_version\": \"…\" },
  \"policy\": { \"pack\": \"dc_standard\", \"version\": \"1.0\",
                \"hash\": \"…\", \"drift_status\": \"none|patch|minor|
                major|unknown|unapproved\" },
  \"target_database\": { \"kind\": \"postgres\", \"version\": \"18\",
                         \"compatibility_floor\": \"16\" },
  \"dbt_validation\": { \"mode\": \"none|dbt|dbt-core|dbt-fusion|
                        docker\", \"available\": bool,
                        \"detected_version\": \"…\" },
  \"generators\": [ { \"name\": \"…\", \"version\": \"…\",
                      \"status\": \"healthy|warning|error\" } ],
  \"dashboard\": { \"asset_status\": \"…\", \"api_compat\": \"…\" },
  \"issues\": { \"unresolved_count\": N },
  \"last_generation\": { \"manifest_hash\": \"…\", \"finished_at\": \"…\",
                         \"result\": \"success|failure\" },
  \"security_warnings\": [ { \"code\": \"…\", \"message\": \"…\" } ],
  \"findings\": [ { \"severity\": \"info|warning|error\",
                    \"code\": \"…\", \"message\": \"…\" } ]
}

Schema lives at docs/dev/api/doctor-schema.json (JSON Schema 2020-12).
Versioned via doctor_schema_version. Breaking changes require an
ADR + version bump (PRD §9.5 spirit).

Acceptance additions (extend the original list):
6. Schema committed at docs/dev/api/doctor-schema.json; CI validates
   that `know-now doctor --format json` output on the demo fixture
   conforms to the schema.
7. Golden tests under tests/doctor/golden/{healthy,warnings,errors}/:
   each captures a fixture project, runs doctor, snapshots both the
   envelope structure (presence of fields) and the severity counts
   (\"healthy: 0 warnings, 0 errors\" etc.). Snapshots tolerate
   non-deterministic fields by redaction (timestamps, hostnames).
8. Support bundle round-trip test: hrw.8 ingests the doctor JSON,
   re-serializes it into the bundle archive, and asserts no field
   loss + no value mutation.
9. Dashboard health view (hrw.19) consumes the schema; a contract
   test runs the doctor → schema → typed TS client → rendered view
   pipeline against the demo fixture and compares to a Playwright
   snapshot (NFR-I8 / NFR-M6).
10. Performance: doctor on the 100-entity demo project completes in
    <500 ms cold (sub-budget vs CLI startup NFR-P1) so it remains
    cheap to run from the dashboard.

title: explain command — artifact / metadata-object / error-code traces
description:
explain answers "why does this exist / why is this warning here?" using
the manifest's traceability data.

PRD refs: §12.7, GEN-012, §22 build sequence #28.

Examples (PRD §12.7):
  know-now explain generated/ddl/postgres/schema.sql
  know-now explain ent_customer
  know-now explain META-REL-001

Reported items (PRD §12.7):
- related metadata objects
- affected artifacts
- generator name + version
- policy rules involved
- template pack inputs (Phase 3+)
- validation gates that ran
- manifest + lockfile hashes
- source YAML spans
- artifact spans
- inferred/defaulted vs. explicit metadata

## Acceptance Criteria
1. explain on an artifact path shows generator, metadata objects,
   policy rules, template inputs, validation gates, and source spans.
2. explain on a metadata object ID shows affected artifacts.
3. Dashboard artifact pages expose the same traceability data.
4. JSON output for CI / editor integrations.

Do NOT:
- Make explain require regeneration. It works against the existing
  manifest; if the manifest is stale, explain says so and points at
  generate.

title: support bundle command — sanitized diagnostic archive
description:
Create a sanitized support bundle for debugging: doctor output, command
logs, manifest, config summary, compatibility status, lockfile hash,
policy/template versions, sanitized diagnostics, recent run summaries.

PRD refs: §12.8, ADMIN-007, NFR-S18.

Output: a zip/tar archive (default zip; --format tar opt-in).

Bundle contents (PRD §12.8):
- doctor output (JSON)
- command logs from .knownow/runs/
- generated/manifest.json (hash only by default; full content opt-in)
- config summary
- compatibility status
- lockfile hash
- policy/template versions
- sanitized diagnostics
- recent run summaries

Redaction (PRD §12.8, NFR-S18):
- Documented allowlist/denylist for env vars, paths, config keys,
  command logs.
- Default-deny on env vars; explicit allowlist for non-sensitive ones
  (USER, OS, ARCH, etc.).
- Path redaction: replace home dir with "$HOME", remove host names.
- Secrets pattern matching: high-entropy tokens, AWS-style key prefixes,
  PEM blocks.
- Metadata inclusion is OPT-IN (--include-metadata).
- Bundle includes a manifest of file categories WITHOUT exposing
  redacted values.
- Sensitive governance metadata is summarized (counts, classifications)
  unless explicitly opted-in.
- Users can preview bundle contents before writing
  (--dry-run prints the included file list).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. support bundle creates an archive with the specified contents.
2. Metadata inclusion opt-in.
3. Secrets and env vars redacted.
4. Bundle includes know-now version, OS/arch, lockfile hash,
   policy/template versions, last generation result.
5. Users can preview (--dry-run).

Do NOT:
- Default to including any user metadata or content from generated/
  full files.
- Send the bundle anywhere automatically; the user transmits it.

title: policy status + policy explain commands
description:
Two policy commands: policy status reports the configured-vs-locked-vs-
approved version triple plus drift class; policy explain documents an
individual finding code.

PRD refs: §12.1, §14.6, §14.7, ADMIN-005, ADMIN-006, §22 build
sequence #29.

policy status (PRD §14.6):
- configured policy versions
- locked policy versions
- available policy versions
- approved policy versions (via approved-version catalog if configured)
- drift status: none / patch / minor / major / unknown / unapproved

policy explain <code> (PRD §14.7):
- rule name
- rule rationale
- severity
- affected object (when run from a project context)
- remediation examples
- organization-specific explanation text where configured

## Acceptance Criteria
1. policy status reports configured/locked/available/approved.
2. Drift classifications enumerated (PRD §14.6).
3. JSON output aggregatable across repos by external scripts.
4. policy explain shows rule/rationale/severity/remediation.
5. Dashboard policy view links to explain output.

Do NOT:
- Mutate policy pack files from explain (PRD §14.4).
- Treat unknown policy features as silently OK in --locked mode
  (META-010 acceptance #6).

title: Epic — Phase 5: advanced intelligence and expanded targets (deferred)
description:
Phase 5 expands know-now beyond Phase 4 with advanced modeling, semantic
automation, additional data platform targets, and stronger sandboxing.
Deliberately deferred — children of this epic are placeholders that
capture intent, not active scope. Phase 5 should NOT start until Phase
4 has shipped and external user feedback has shaped scope.

PRD refs: §6.6 (Phase 5 candidate scope), §15.3 (WASI plugin runtime),
§23.5 (Phase 4 entry criteria — implicit Phase 5 entry: Phase 4 stable).

Phase 5 should NOT start until:
- Phase 4 is stable (qas children all closed or explicitly deferred
  again).
- Real-world demand for the Phase 5 candidates is documented from at
  least 2 production engagements.
- The metadata model has survived enough Phase 4 / hosted feedback
  that schema changes are slow.
- ADRs exist for: AI-assisted-editing safety model, WASI sandboxing
  model, Data Vault / MDM scope boundary.

Candidate scope (PRD §6.6):
- Natural language metadata input.
- AI-assisted metadata editing (explicit, reviewable workflows; the
  AI never writes to metadata/ without human accept).
- Data Vault 2.0 generation.
- OSI-compatible metrics layer generation.
- Master Data Management with probabilistic matching + steward queues.
- Reference data management.
- Business Vault SQL editor.
- Schema-aware autocomplete (beyond the JSON Schema autocomplete that
  ships in Phase 2A).
- Reconciliation SQL generation.
- Rich synthetic data generation from semantic types + constraints
  (beyond the basic 8vf.6 fixture data).
- WASI plugin runtime for stronger sandboxing (replaces / complements
  the in-process restricted MiniJinja profile from Phase 3).
- Optional hosted collaboration features (extends Phase 4 hosted/
  control-plane).

Hard rules (carry over from prior phases):
- Local-first remains the default. Any Phase 5 hosted feature is
  opt-in, documented, and reversible (PRD §6.5 control-plane boundary).
- AI-assisted editing follows PRD §10.11 metadata-writing rules:
  preview, backup, --apply opt-in, comments + formatting preserved.
- WASI plugins replace MiniJinja templates only when the WASI runtime
  is at least as restricted as the existing renderer profile (NFR-S22
  ..S25 invariants must hold or be strengthened, never weakened).
- No telemetry by default (PRD §4.4).
- Generator contract changes from new targets (Data Vault, metrics)
  go through the standard versioning + ADR + migration-note workflow
  (AGENTS.md §9.4).

Children placeholders cover the candidate scope above; each is P4 +
deferred and explicitly blocked on hrw.26 (Phase 3 exit) and qas.7
(hosted control-plane track) where applicable.

## Success Criteria

Close this epic only when its child beads prove the described scope, every phase or NFR gate is linked to evidence, and remaining follow-up work is explicitly deferred into separate beads.

title: Natural language metadata input (deferred — Phase 5)
description:
Natural language metadata input: parse user-written prose ("we have
customers, orders, and products; orders belong to customers via
customer_id") into draft AuthoringMetadata YAML the user reviews +
commits. Deferred to Phase 5 — the safety model + UX must be solid
before anything writes to metadata/.

PRD refs: §6.6 (Phase 5 candidate scope — natural language metadata
input), §10.11 (preservation requirements for any metadata-writing).

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 4 stable.
- ADR records the NL parsing approach: deterministic grammar (e.g.,
  Lark-style) vs. LLM-driven vs. hybrid. The default for the engine
  must be local-first (no required cloud calls); LLM-backed extraction
  is opt-in via explicit configuration + an isolated adapter.
- hrw.12 / qas.9 / qas.10 YAML patcher is production-quality so
  generated drafts compose with existing metadata cleanly.

Hard rules:
- Output is ALWAYS a draft for human review. Never auto-applied.
- Local-first by default (NFR-CLI-007). LLM-backed extraction must be
  configurable + opt-in with a clear network/cost warning.
- LLM-backed adapters never see the user's metadata in plaintext
  unless the user explicitly enables it (redaction-on-by-default).
- Generated drafts respect the YAML authoring subset (§10.2) — the
  same emitter as the rest of know-now.
- Backups + previews mandatory (PRD §10.11).

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Business Vault SQL editor (deferred — Phase 5)
description:

shipped or at least scoped, since Business Vault sits on top of
Raw Vault.

PRD refs: §6.6 (Phase 5 candidate scope — Business Vault SQL editor).

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 4 stable. Pilot demand for Data Vault modeling.
- kn8.3 (Data Vault 2.0 generation) is shipped or has an accepted
  ADR establishing the Raw Vault contract on which Business Vault
  builds.
- ADR records: where Business Vault SQL lives (custom/business_vault/
  vs. generated/), how the editor surfaces (CLI assist, dashboard
  panel, both), and how authored SQL is validated against the
  metadata contract.
- New crate know_now_business_vault (NFR-M1) OR the editor is a
  dashboard-only feature backed by the API.

Hard rules:
- Authored Business Vault SQL is user-owned (PRD §4.3 ownership).
  The editor assists, validates, and previews; it never overwrites
  user SQL without an explicit --apply, mirroring PRD §10.11
  metadata-write rules.
- Generated artifacts (DDL, dbt models) referenced by the editor
  remain owned by the engine; the editor reads but does not write

- Authored SQL is parse-validated (sqlparser-rs) before save.
- No network calls; the editor is local-first (PRD §4.4).

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Auto-generating business rules from natural language (kn8.1

title: Rich synthetic data generation from semantic types + constraints (deferred — Phase 5)
description:
Rich synthetic data generation that extends 8vf.6's deterministic
demo fixture data into a full-featured generator: semantic-type-aware
distributions, relationship cardinality preservation, governance-aware
PII shaping, scale-target sizing, and reproducible seeds. Deferred to
Phase 5 — the basic deterministic fixture data in 8vf.6 covers Phase
2B examples; rich synthetic data is a separate scope that needs its
own demand validation.

PRD refs: §6.6 (Phase 5 candidate scope — rich synthetic data
generation from semantic types + constraints; explicitly distinct
from 8vf.6 basic fixture data per the kn8 epic body), §10.8 (semantic
type influence on synthetic data — listed under 'future synthetic /
test data generation').

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 4 stable.
- 8vf.6 (deterministic demo fixture data) shipped + has user
  feedback on what 'rich' means in practice.
- ADR records: distribution sources (built-in semantic-type
  generators, optional user-supplied generators via declarative
  template-pack-like manifests, NEVER arbitrary code), reproducibility
  posture (seed + manifest hash → byte-identical output, NFR-R1),
  and the privacy posture for governance-classified attributes
  (PII attributes get realistic-shaped but synthetic values; no real
  data).
- New crate know_now_gen_synthetic (NFR-M1 / NFR-SC2).

Hard rules:
- Synthetic data clearly labeled as synthetic (PRD §11.9).
- Production projects do NOT generate synthetic data unless
  explicitly configured (PRD §11.9).
- Reproducible: identical metadata + seed → byte-identical output
  (NFR-R1).
- Generators are declarative + sandboxed (mirrors PRD §15.1
  template-pack posture); no arbitrary code execution.
- PII attributes get realistic-shaped synthetic values; never real
  data (NFR-S4 spirit).

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Loading external datasets and resampling them (privacy /
  licensing review required first).
- ML-driven generation (would compose with kn8.2 AI track,
  separate safety model).

title: WASI plugin runtime for stronger sandboxing (deferred — Phase 5)
description:
WASI plugin runtime for stronger sandboxing of custom generators +
template extensions. Deferred to Phase 5 — the restricted MiniJinja
profile (Phase 3) covers most safe extensibility; WASI gives stronger
isolation guarantees but requires substantial runtime work.

PRD refs: §6.6 (Phase 5 candidate scope — WASI plugin runtime), §15.3
(Phase 5 WASI plugin runtime), §15.1 (Phase 3 declarative template
packs — strict / fuel-limited / path-isolated baseline that WASI
must match or exceed), §15.2 (Phase 4 external generator protocol —
JSON stdin/stdout — orthogonal but related), AGENTS.md §4.4, §8.

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 4 stable. External generator protocol (qas.6) has shipped + is
  stable.
- ADR records the WASI runtime choice: wasmtime, wasmer, or other,
  with full cargo deny audit + dependency posture review.
- ADR records the host capability surface: explicitly enumerated
  (read pack root / write artifact descriptors only / no env / no
  net / no DB / no process). MUST be at least as restricted as the
  Phase 3 know-now-minijinja-v1 profile (NFR-S22..S25). Anything less
  restrictive is a regression that fitness tests reject.
- Compat-fixture suite covers untrusted-WASI-in-locked-mode failure
  (parallel to the template-pack fixtures in PRD §20.2).

Hard rules:
- WASI plugins return artifact descriptors only. They never write
  files directly. The artifact writer remains the single write path
  (AGENTS.md §4.1).
- Plugins cannot register native host functions beyond the explicitly
  allowlisted set (NFR-S22 spirit).
- Plugins cannot read environment variables, execute processes, open
  network connections, or access databases (NFR-S25 spirit).
- Lockfile records plugin name + version + content hash + WASI runtime
  version (carry-over from Phase 3 template-pack lockfile entries).
- Trust levels enumerated: built-in / approved / experimental /
  untrusted (parallel to template packs).

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Hot-reloading plugins.
- Inter-plugin communication.
- GPU / hardware access.

title: Metadata schema migration tooling (deferred)
description:
Metadata schema migration tooling: when know-now's metadata schema
version bumps (1.0 → 1.1 etc.), users need a deterministic, reviewable
migration that updates their metadata/ tree to the new schema.
Deferred to Phase 4 because schema migrations only become necessary
once metadata_schema_version actually breaks (Phase 1-3 is 1.0 / 1.x).

PRD refs: §6.5 (Phase 4 candidate scope — metadata schema migration
tooling), §10.11 (preservation requirements), AGENTS.md §9.4
(versioned compatibility surfaces).

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 3 stable.
- A real metadata_schema_version bump is on the roadmap (the tool is
  vacuous before then).
- ADR records the migration model: sequential migrations 1.0 → 1.1 →
  1.2 (one upgrade per version) vs. consolidated migrations vs. lock-
  step with engine version.
- hrw.12 (id backfill --apply) YAML patcher is production-quality
  (same dependency as qas.9).

Candidate scope (deferred):
- `know-now metadata migrate --from 1.0 --to 1.1 --dry-run`
- `know-now metadata migrate --from 1.0 --to 1.1 --apply`
- Each migration is a declarative recipe in the engine binary,
  versioned and tested via fixture diffs.
- Migration failure preserves the prior metadata/ tree atomically
  (same posture as the artifact writer).
- Migration registers in the manifest's migration history field
  (Phase 4 manifest extension; ADR required).

Hard rules:
- Metadata writes follow PRD §10.11 (preservation, backups, previews,
  --apply opt-in).
- No automatic migrations on validate / check / generate. Migration
  is always an explicit command.
- Backups under .knownow/backups/migration-<from>-to-<to>-<ts>/.
- Cross-project migrations remain out of scope (single project at a
  time; admin scan reports schema-version drift across projects).

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Migrating between major engine versions (covered by the engine
  upgrade workflow, not this tool).
- Migrating policy/template packs (those have their own version
  fields).

## Acceptance Criteria
- ADR records the migration model.
- Each migration has a fixture pair (pre + post) with snapshot tests.
- Migration parser uses the same YAML patcher as hrw.12 / qas.9 —
  no separate parser stack.

title: Lineage tracking via generated + parsed SQL (deferred)
description:
Lineage tracking via generated SQL (DDL + dbt models) and parsed
custom SQL. Records column-level provenance from source systems
through staging + marts to consumer artifacts. Deferred to Phase 4
because lineage requires substantial SQL parsing + a stable
generator-contract surface, both of which only stabilize after Phase 3
ships.

PRD refs: §6.5 (Phase 4 candidate scope — lineage tracking via
generated and parsed SQL), AGENTS.md §4.4 (no raw SQL interpolation —
lineage parser uses sqlparser-rs).

Pre-conditions:
- Phase 3 stable (S_P3 features all green).
- Stable generator contract (no breaking changes pending).
- Decision (ADR) on lineage representation: column-level vs.
  table-level vs. both, and the storage format
  (.knownow/lineage.json vs. a separate generated artifact).

Candidate scope (deferred):
- Parse generated DDL + dbt SQL via sqlparser-rs to derive lineage
  edges (source.col → staging.col → mart.col).
- Parse user-owned custom/dbt/ models via the same parser to extend
  lineage past the generated boundary.
- Surface lineage in:
  - dashboard entity-detail view (upstream + downstream column maps).
  - explain command (artifact → lineage trace).
  - admin scan (project-level lineage health summary).
- Export lineage as OpenLineage-compatible JSON for downstream tools
  (where practical; pinned schema version).

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Real-time lineage from running databases (introspection in qas.4
  must ship first).
- Cross-project lineage federation.
- ML-driven lineage inference for ambiguous SQL.

Hard rules (carry over from Phase 3 invariants):
- Lineage parsing is read-only over generated/ + custom/. Never
  mutates user files.
- Custom SQL parsing is best-effort; failures degrade gracefully
  (lineage marked "partial" in dashboard + manifest).
- No telemetry; lineage data lives under .knownow/lineage.json (or
  configured local path).

## Acceptance Criteria
- ADR records the lineage representation + storage format.
- Lineage parser's SQL parser dependency choice goes through cargo
  deny.
- Dashboard view honors WCAG 2.2 AA (carry over from hrw.21).

title: Rule-based bulk metadata transformations (deferred)
description:
Rule-based bulk metadata transformations: declarative rewrites that
operate on a known canonical graph slice (e.g., "rename every
attribute matching `*_dt` to `*_at`", "add governance.classification
to every entity in domain customer that currently lacks it"). Deferred
to Phase 4 because bulk transformations require the YAML patcher from
hrw.12 (id backfill --apply) to be production-quality first.

PRD refs: §6.5 (Phase 4 candidate scope — rule-based bulk metadata
transformations), §10.11 (preservation requirements for any future
metadata-writing).

Pre-conditions:
- hrw.12 (id backfill --apply) has shipped a robust YAML patcher
  that preserves comments + formatting and writes backups.
- ADR records the bulk-transformation rule DSL (declarative; no code
  execution — same posture as policy packs and template packs).
- Backups + previews are required, not optional.

Candidate rule kinds (deferred):
- Rename attribute / entity / relationship by pattern.
- Add / remove governance fields by predicate.
- Move attributes between entities (with rename + backref update).
- Apply a default to every object missing a field.
- Snake_case canonicalization sweep.

Hard rules:
- Bulk transformations are an explicit metadata-writing command (PRD
  §10.11). They never run from validate / check / generate / diff
  etc.
- Every transformation produces a unified-diff preview before write.
- Backups under .knownow/backups/<ts>/ for every modified file.
- --dry-run is the default; --apply is opt-in (same posture as
  hrw.12).
- Rule expressions are declarative-only (no code execution), parsed
  via the same family of safety constraints as policy packs.

Out-of-scope until pre-conditions:
- Imperative scripting / arbitrary code execution.
- Running across projects (single project at a time).
- AI-suggested transformations (Phase 5).

## Acceptance Criteria
- ADR records the rule DSL.
- Tests cover BOM-prefixed, leading-comments, mixed-indent fixtures
  (carry over from hrw.12).
- After apply: validate, check, generate must succeed without further
  user action.

title: Epic — Phase 1: architecture contract spike
description:
Phase 1 proves the core architecture before building a full product surface.
Deliberately scoped to a thin vertical slice that exercises every load-bearing
contract: parser → typed metadata → ProjectGraph → versioned generator
contract → deterministic writer → manifest → one PostgreSQL DDL artifact and
one Markdown documentation artifact.

PRD refs: §6.1 (Phase 1 scope), §22 build sequence #2-#6, §23.1 (exit criteria).

Excluded (do NOT pull these in here): full CLI command set, dashboard, dbt
generation, policy packs, template packs, diffing, incremental generation,
external generator protocol, metadata rewriting (PRD §6.1 Excluded).

Exit criteria (PRD §23.1):
- Metadata parses with source spans.
- Parser spike proves serde-saphyr can enforce the know-now YAML subset
  directly or through an explicit pre-scan/event validation layer (else
  switch to marked-yaml fallback per PRD §10.2).
- Unsupported YAML features and duplicate keys produce source-aware
  diagnostics.
- Parser file-size, nesting, and expansion limits are tested.
- Graph validation catches at least 10 meaningful errors.
- Generator never reads YAML directly.
- Generated artifacts are deterministic across consecutive runs and contain
  no volatile timestamps.
- Atomic write proof-of-concept preserves prior output on failure.
- Path safety rejects writes outside generated roots.
- Metadata model is expressive enough for one realistic consulting-style
  fixture (≥8 entities, multiple source mappings, semantic/logical types,
  domains/modules, relationships, open questions, governance metadata).

Architecture invariants enforced from day one (AGENTS.md §4):
- Generators never read YAML; they consume only the validated GeneratorContract.
- Parser dependencies are isolated to know_now_metadata.
- Built-in generators do not write files directly.
- Identical input produces byte-identical deterministic output.
- Volatile run state lives under .knownow/ only.

## Success Criteria

Close this epic only when its child beads prove the described scope, every phase or NFR gate is linked to evidence, and remaining follow-up work is explicitly deferred into separate beads.

title: Demo metadata fixture — ≥8 entities, multi-source, governance, questions
description:
Author the realistic consulting-style metadata fixture that Phase 1 must
parse, validate, and generate from. Without this fixture, the Phase 1 spike
is academic.

PRD refs: §6.1 (Phase 1 Included — one realistic demo metadata model),
§23.1 (exit criteria — at least one consulting-style fixture), §10
(metadata model), §10.4-§10.10 (entity/relationship/source/governance
shapes), LIFE-002 (demo project).

Required content (all in the YAML authoring subset per §10.2):
- ≥8 entities. Suggested ecommerce baseline: customer, order, product,
  inventory, supplier, return, customer_segment, address.
- Multiple source mappings (≥2 sources, ≥3 tables) per §10.6.
- Semantic types covering: identifier, name, email, phone, currency_amount,
  status, country_code, postal_code, created_timestamp, updated_timestamp.
- Logical types covering: string, integer, decimal (with precision+scale),
  boolean, date, timestamp (with timezone semantics), uuid, json.
- ≥1 self-referencing relationship (e.g., customer parent/child).
- ≥1 many-to-many relationship (e.g., customer ↔ customer_segment).
- Domains + modules (≥2 domains, ≥2 modules per domain).
- Governance metadata: owner, steward, classification, sensitivity, pii,
  retention_policy on at least 3 entities.
- ≥3 open questions (one with `status: needs_stakeholder_confirmation`,
  one blocking).
- ≥2 assumptions with confidence levels.
- Stable IDs on every object using the §8.8 prefix convention
  (dom_, mod_, ent_, attr_, rel_, src_, rule_, pol_).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Fixture lives at fixtures/demo_ecommerce/metadata/.
2. All YAML files use only the §10.2 subset (no anchors, aliases, merge
   keys, custom tags, includes, multi-doc files, duplicate keys).
3. Fixture parses without errors once the parser spike lands.
4. Fixture exercises every metadata construct that Phase 1 generators
   touch (so the snapshot tests can prove coverage).
5. README.md inside fixture explains its provenance and what features it
   exercises (so future contributors can extend it without breaking
   coverage).

Do NOT:
- Include real PII or client data.
- Use prefixes outside the documented set.

title: First PostgreSQL DDL artifact — single entity table
description:
Phase 1's vertical slice through the codegen path: a single PostgreSQL
table for one entity from the demo fixture, written via the artifact
writer, recorded in the manifest, deterministic across runs.

PRD refs: §6.1 (Phase 1 Included — one PG DDL artifact), §8.9 (DDL
generation architecture), §11.2 (PostgreSQL DDL spec), GEN-001, §22
build sequence #6, §23.1 (exit criteria).

Pipeline (PRD §8.9):
  ProjectGraph
    -> GeneratorContract
    -> LogicalSchema
    -> DialectDdlIr
    -> DeterministicSqlEmitter
    -> Parser validation
    -> Optional live database execution validation
    -> Artifact set

Phase 1 scope (intentionally minimal):
- Single CREATE TABLE statement for one entity.
- Columns derived from attributes (logical type → PG type via the simplest
  default mapping; semantic types do not yet drive type mapping).
- Primary key from business_key.
- NOT NULL from required: true.
- No foreign keys yet (Phase 2A).
- No indexes, check constraints, comments yet (Phase 2A).

Architecture invariants (AGENTS.md §4.4, NFR-S1, NFR-S2):
- Use typed DDL IR (know_now_ir). NO raw SQL string interpolation for
  identifiers or literals (NFR-S1, AGENTS.md §8 hard don't).
- Validate metadata identifiers before DDL IR construction (NFR-S2).
- Generated SQL is parse-validated before write (use sqlparser-rs or
  equivalent; cite the dependency choice in cargo deny).
- Generator never reads YAML (AGENTS.md §4.1; fitness test enforces).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. know_now_gen_postgres crate exists with a generator trait
   implementation.
2. Output file at generated/ddl/postgres/schema.sql carries the
   ownership header (PRD §11.8).
3. SQL parses successfully.
4. Two consecutive runs produce byte-identical schema.sql.
5. Manifest entry for the artifact lists generator, generator_version,
   metadata_object_ids ({entity_id, attribute_ids...}), and trace
   spans (artifact line range -> metadata object id).
6. Fitness test: know_now_gen_postgres has no path to know_now_metadata
   in its dep graph (only know_now_contract / know_now_codegen /
   know_now_ir).

title: First Markdown documentation artifact — single entity page
description:
Phase 1's second vertical slice: a single Markdown documentation page for
one entity, written via the artifact writer, recorded in the manifest,
deterministic across runs.

PRD refs: §6.1 (Phase 1 Included — one Markdown documentation artifact),
§11.5 (documentation generation), GEN-004, §22 build sequence #6.

Phase 1 scope (intentionally minimal):
- One entity page at generated/docs/entities/<entity_name>.md.
- Title, plain-language summary, attribute table (name | logical type |
  semantic type | required | description).
- Ownership header in HTML comment form
  (<!-- Generated by know-now ... -->) — PRD §11.8.
- No relationships, ER diagram, governance section, open-question list,
  or assumptions list yet (Phase 2A/2B).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. know_now_gen_docs crate exists with a generator trait
   implementation.
2. Output file renders correctly in GitHub Markdown viewer
   (manual-checked once; thereafter snapshot test).
3. Two consecutive runs produce byte-identical .md.
4. Manifest entry includes metadata_object_ids and trace spans.
5. No relative links to files that may not exist (validate links if
   tooling is cheap; otherwise punt to Phase 2A).
6. Fitness test: know_now_gen_docs has no path to know_now_metadata.

Do NOT:
- Use HTML beyond comments. Markdown only (PRD §11.5).
- Embed timestamps in the page.
- Embed self-hash of the file content (AGENTS.md §8).

title: Phase 1 deterministic snapshot test (byte-identical proof)
description:
The Phase 1 acceptance gate: a snapshot test that runs the demo fixture
through the full pipeline and asserts byte-identical generated output
across two consecutive runs and across supported OSes.

PRD refs: §23.1 (exit criteria), GEN-007, NFR-R1, NFR-R3, AGENTS.md §4.2.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Test loads fixtures/demo_ecommerce/, runs parse → graph → contract
   projection → generation, and writes to a temp dir.
2. Asserts that generated/ddl/postgres/schema.sql, generated/docs/
   entities/<entity>.md, and generated/manifest.json are byte-identical
   on two consecutive runs.
3. Asserts that .knownow/last_generation.json differs only in volatile
   fields (run_id, timestamps, durations).
4. Cross-platform CI runs this test on Linux, macOS, and Windows; the
   test asserts byte-identical output across the three runs (LF line
   endings, UTF-8 encoding — NFR-PO3).
5. The test is the canonical Phase 1 exit-criteria proof; gate the Phase
   2A epic on this passing in CI.

title: Phase 1 exit-criteria audit + gate to Phase 2A
description:
Closing bead for Phase 1. Walk PRD §23.1 line by line, link each criterion
to the bead/test that proves it, and update the AGENTS.md project status
from "pre-implementation" to "Phase 1 complete; Phase 2A active."

PRD refs: §23.1 (exit criteria), AGENTS.md §2 (repository status).

Checklist (each item must link to a passing test or merged bead):
- [ ] minimal project initializes or loads from fixture
- [ ] metadata parses with source spans
- [ ] serde-saphyr (or marked-yaml fallback) enforces the YAML subset
- [ ] unsupported YAML features produce source-aware diagnostics
- [ ] duplicate keys fail with source-aware diagnostics
- [ ] parser file-size, nesting, and resource limits are tested
- [ ] canonical ProjectGraph is built
- [ ] versioned generator contract exists
- [ ] generator capability declaration exists
- [ ] deterministic manifest exists
- [ ] volatile run log is separate from deterministic generated output
- [ ] one PostgreSQL DDL artifact is generated
- [ ] one Markdown documentation artifact is generated
- [ ] manifest records artifact hashes and metadata hash
- [ ] output is deterministic across consecutive runs (proved by upe.12)
- [ ] atomic write proof of concept preserves previous output on failure
- [ ] path safety prevents writes outside generated roots
- [ ] at least one consulting-style fixture is represented successfully
- [ ] structured-logging facade emits the §17.1 stage spans (proved by
       42e.10 + asserted by upe.14)
- [ ] phase-1 e2e shell driver passes (upe.14) — events.jsonl asserts +
       cross-OS check + secret-redaction smoke

## Acceptance Criteria
1. All checklist items linked to bead IDs (this bead's comments).
2. AGENTS.md §2 status updated.
3. PRD §23.1 marked as "complete on <commit-sha>".
4. The Phase 2A epic (know-now-63g) is unblocked from this bead via
   `br dep add know-now-63g <this-bead> --type blocks` (already wired
   when this bead is created — see "Wire cross-epic blocks" task).
5. cargo xtask check + cargo xtask e2e phase-1 (upe.14) green.

title: Phase 1 E2E test script + verbose JSON-log assertions
description:
Phase 1 end-to-end test script with verbose JSON-log assertions.
Complements upe.12 (in-process byte-identical snapshot) by exercising the
full Phase 1 vertical slice from a shell driver, capturing the structured
event stream from 42e.10, and asserting on stage names, durations,
artifact counts, and the absence of forbidden content (timestamps in
manifest, log content in generated/, secrets in .knownow/runs/). Stage
taxonomy is canonical in 42e.10 (docs/dev/logging.md); span names
referenced below are the Phase-1-specific entries within that taxonomy.

PRD refs: §6.1 (Phase 1 scope), §8.5 (generation pipeline stages),
§17.1 (NFR-P12 stage timings), §23.1 (Phase 1 exit criteria), AGENTS.md
§4.2 (volatile vs deterministic).

Why this is separate from upe.12:
- upe.12 proves byte-identical generated/ across two consecutive runs.
- This bead proves the *pipeline* logged what we expected, in the order
  we expected, within the budget we expected, and that what got logged
  doesn't leak into deterministic artifacts. It is the "great, detailed
  logging" half of "comprehensive unit tests + e2e test scripts".

Test driver shape (xtask subcommand `cargo xtask e2e phase-1` plus a
stand-alone shell script under tests/e2e/phase_1/run.sh for documentation
+ debugging; the xtask form is canonical for CI):
  1. Stand up a fresh tempdir.
  2. Copy fixtures/demo_ecommerce/ into the tempdir.
  3. Run the Phase 1 vertical-slice binary (or `know-now check`-style
     internal harness if the CLI is not yet wired) with --verbose
     --format json, redirecting events to events.jsonl and the run
     summary to .knownow/last_generation.json.
  4. Run a second time; capture both events streams.
  5. Run a third time AFTER tampering with one byte of generated/ddl/
     postgres/schema.sql; expect manual-edit detection failure (the PoC
     refuses promotion when previous manifest exists and target file
     differs — upe.9).
  6. Run a fourth time after deleting generated/manifest.json; expect
     a clear warning that manual-edit detection is disabled, and
     successful regeneration.
  7. Run a fifth time injecting a malformed YAML fixture (anchor /
     duplicate key / multi-doc) into metadata/ and expect a single
     parser diagnostic with file/line/column/YAML-path; assert exit
     code is non-zero.

Assertions (per run):
- Event stream covers all stages from PRD §8.5 #1-#7 (parsing,
  yaml-subset, deserialize, semantic, policy*, default-resolution*,
  contract) plus #11-#18 for generation runs. (* policy + default
  resolution may emit a single "no-op" event in Phase 1 since policy
  packs are Phase 2A — assert presence, not work).
- Each stage span has duration_ms and a stable stage name from the
  taxonomy in 42e.10.
- generated/manifest.json contains NO key matching the chrono epoch
  ranges, NO machine hostname, NO username, NO absolute path
  (recursive scan).
- .knownow/last_generation.json DOES contain run_id, started_at,
  finished_at (NFR-R13).
- Across runs 1 and 2, generated/manifest.json is byte-identical and
  generated/ditches duration_ms entirely (cross-check with upe.12).
- The structured log JSON validates against
  know_now_diagnostics::LOG_SCHEMA_VERSION.
- Secret-redaction smoke: inject a fake AWS-style key into the
  tempdir's environment; assert it never appears in events.jsonl,
  .knownow/audit.log (Phase 3 scaffold), or generated/.

Logging detail required:
- Each event includes: ts (volatile, ISO-8601), level, stage, event,
  fields{}, target, message, run_id, span_id, parent_span_id.
- The driver pretty-prints a per-stage timing summary at the end so a
  human reading CI logs can see where time went without parsing
  events.jsonl.
- On failure, the driver dumps the last 200 lines of events.jsonl AND
  the staging dir contents AND the .knownow/staging/<run_id>/ tree to
  CI artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `cargo xtask e2e phase-1` exits 0 on a green Phase 1 build and
   non-zero (with diagnostic) on regressions.
2. Test runtime <30 s on the reference CI machine.
3. CI uploads events.jsonl + last_generation.json + the staging dir as
   artifacts on failure for post-mortem.
4. Cross-platform: same script (or xtask) runs on Linux, macOS, Windows
   (NFR-PO1). Windows differences (line endings in diagnostics,
   symlink-skip) are documented.
5. Documentation: tests/e2e/phase_1/README.md walks a contributor
   through running the driver locally and interpreting the output.
6. The bead's success is itself an exit-criteria item on upe.13.

Do NOT:
- Inline the assertions inside the production binary; this is a test
  harness, not a runtime concern.
- Snapshot duration_ms values — they are volatile by design. Snapshot
  only stage names + ordering + presence of fields.
- Use println! in the driver — go through the same tracing facade
  (42e.10) so the harness output is itself structured.

title: serde-saphyr parser/deserializer spike + YAML subset enforcement
description:
Phase 1 load-bearing spike. Decide whether serde-saphyr can enforce the
know-now YAML authoring subset (§10.2) directly or via a pre-scan/event
validation layer. If it cannot, switch to marked-yaml fallback.

PRD refs: §10.2 (YAML authoring subset + parser requirements + fallback
rule), §22 build sequence #2, §23.1 (exit criteria), NFR-S13, NFR-S19,
NFR-S21, NFR-M8, NFR-R14, AGENTS.md §4.5 (banned deps).

Parser requirements (PRD §10.2):
- Source spans for diagnostics.
- Typed Serde-compatible deserialization into Rust metadata structs.
- Deterministic mapping/sequence handling.
- Configurable file-size, nesting, and resource-exhaustion limits.
- Duplicate-key detection and rejection.
- Clear errors for unsupported YAML features.
- Pre-scan or event-level validation that rejects unsupported features
  before metadata reaches semantic validation.
- Diagnostic output with file, line, column, YAML path where available,
  and suggested remediation for common unsupported syntax.

Phase 1/2 YAML subset (PRD §10.2 — REJECT all of these):
- anchors, aliases, merge keys, custom YAML tags
- include directives
- multi-document metadata files
- duplicate keys
- excessive nesting
- excessive file sizes
- ambiguous scalar forms

Implementation shape (PRD §10.2):
  YAML text
    -> syntax parse with serde-saphyr
    -> know-now YAML subset validation
    -> typed authoring metadata structs with source spans
    -> semantic validation
    -> ProjectGraph

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Parser dependency lives ONLY in know_now_metadata. Fitness test
   verifies (NFR-M8, AGENTS.md §4.1).
2. Each banned construct produces a source-aware diagnostic (file, line,
   column, YAML path, error code, remediation hint).
3. Duplicate-key diagnostic includes both the duplicate location and the
   original key location (META-003 acceptance #5).
4. File size, nesting depth, and expansion limits are configurable via
   ParserBudgets and have unit tests proving they trigger before OOM.
5. ParsedMetadataDocument struct exists with source_id, path, version,
   document, spans (per §10.2 example).
6. Fallback assessment: if any subset enforcement requires kludgy custom
   re-parsing, document why and switch to marked-yaml. Record the
   decision as an ADR (AGENTS.md §7.1).

Do NOT:
- Add a YAML parser dependency to any other crate (AGENTS.md §4.1, §4.5).
- Use serde_yaml or serde_yml under any circumstances (banned —
  AGENTS.md §4.5).
- Bring in a C/FFI YAML parser (NFR-S5, AGENTS.md §4.5).
- Allow parser configuration that lets unsupported constructs through
  silently.
